title.zh: 卷三十七 桓榮丁鴻列傳
title.en: Volume 37: Biographies of Huan Rong, Ding Hong
title.raw: 卷三十七 桓榮丁鴻列傳 Volume 37: Biographies of Huan Rong, Ding Hong

sentences.zh: 後漢書卷三十七 桓榮丁鴻列傳 第二十七 桓榮字春卿，沛郡龍亢人也。

translations.literal: Book of Later Han, scroll 37, collective biography number 27 of Huan Rong and Ding Hong: Huan Rong, courtesy name Chunqing, was a man of Longkang in the Pei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: From the Book of Later Han, scroll 37: the twenty-seventh set of paired biographies, of Huan Rong and Ding Hong. Huan Rong, courtesy name Chunqing, came from Longkang in Pei commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]少學長安，習歐陽尚書，事博士九江朱普。

translations.literal: [One] In youth he studied in Chang'an, practiced the Ouyang school's Documents of the Classics, and served as a pupil of the erudite Zhu Pu of Jiujiang.
translations.idiomatic: As a young man he studied in Chang'an, mastering the Ouyang tradition of the Book of Documents under the court scholar Zhu Pu of Jiujiang.

sentences.zh: [二] 貧窶無資，[三]常客傭以自給，精力不倦，十五年不窺家園。

translations.literal: [Two] Poor and without resources, [three] he often hired himself out as a laborer to support himself; his energy did not tire, and for fifteen years he did not glance toward his home garden.
translations.idiomatic: Destitute and without means, he often worked as a hired hand to get by; he never flagged, and for fifteen years he never so much as looked homeward.

sentences.zh: 至王莽篡位乃歸。

translations.literal: Only when Wang Mang usurped the throne did he return.
translations.idiomatic: It was not until Wang Mang seized the throne that he went home.

sentences.zh: 會朱普卒，榮奔喪九江，負土成墳，因留教授，徒觿數百人。

translations.literal: When Zhu Pu died, Rong hurried to Jiujiang for the mourning, carried earth to complete the grave mound, and on that account remained to teach; his disciples numbered several hundred.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhu Pu died, Huan Rong rushed to Jiujiang to mourn him, shouldering earth until the tomb was heaped high, then stayed on to teach. Several hundred students gathered at his belt.

sentences.zh: 莽敗，天下亂。

translations.literal: Mang was defeated; the realm was in chaos.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang fell, and the empire descended into turmoil.

sentences.zh: 榮抱其經書與弟子逃匿山谷，雖常饑困而講論不輟，後復客授江淮閒。

translations.literal: Rong embraced his classics and with his disciples fled and hid in mountain valleys; though often hungry and distressed, he did not cease lecturing and discussion, and afterward again as a guest teacher instructed between the Yangzi and the Huai.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Rong clutched his texts and fled into the hills with his students; even when they were half starved he never stopped expounding the classics. Later he resumed itinerant teaching between the Yangzi and the Huai.

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢書曰：「榮本齊人，遷於龍亢，至榮六葉。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] the Continued Book of Han states: "Rong was originally a man of Qi; he moved to Longkang; down to Rong it was six generations."
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Continued Book of Han says that Huan Rong's family were originally from Qi, relocated to Longkang, and that six generations had passed down to Huan Rong himself.

sentences.zh: 」東觀記曰：「榮本齊桓公後也。

translations.literal: " The Eastern Lodge Record states: "Rong was originally a descendant of Duke Huan of Qi."
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Lodge Record adds that he was descended from Duke Huan of Qi.

sentences.zh: 桓公作伯，支庶用其謚立族命氏焉。」

translations.literal: When the duke acted as hegemon, collateral lines used his posthumous title to establish their clan and receive their surname therefrom."
translations.idiomatic: When that duke rose to hegemony, junior branches took his posthumous title as the basis of their lineage and adopted it as their clan name."

sentences.zh: 注[二]朱普字公文，受業於平當，為博士，徒觿尤盛。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] Zhu Pu, courtesy name Gongwen, received instruction from Ping Dang, became an erudite, and his disciples were especially numerous.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Zhu Pu, courtesy name Gongwen, had studied under Ping Dang, held a chair as court scholar, and drew an exceptionally large following of students.

sentences.zh: 見前書。

translations.literal: See the former book.
translations.idiomatic: See the earlier Han history.

sentences.zh: 注[三]字林曰：「窶，空也。」

translations.literal: Commentary [three] the Forest of Graphs states: "Ju means empty."
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: The Forest of Graphs glosses ju (want) as "empty, destitute."

sentences.zh: 建武十九年，年六十餘，始辟大司徒府。

translations.literal: In the nineteenth year of Jianwu, when he was over sixty, he was first summoned to the Grand Excellency of Education's office.
translations.idiomatic: In 43 CE, already past sixty, he received his first appointment to the staff of the Grand Excellency of Education.

sentences.zh: 時顯宗始立為皇太子，選求明經，乃擢榮弟子豫章何湯為虎賁中郎將，以尚書授太子。

translations.literal: At that time the Manifest Ancestor had just been established as heir apparent; they selected and sought those clear in the classics, and therefore promoted Rong's disciple He Tang of Yuzhang to Gentlemen-of-the-Household Rapid as Tigers, to use the Documents to instruct the heir.
translations.idiomatic: The future Emperor Ming had just been named crown prince, and the court wanted a scholar steeped in the classics. Huan Rong's disciple He Tang of Yuzhang was therefore promoted to captain of the Rapid-as-Tigers guard and charged with teaching the prince the Book of Documents.

sentences.zh: 世祖從容問湯[一]本師為誰，湯對曰：「事沛國桓榮。

translations.literal: The Founding Ancestor calmly asked Tang [one] who his original teacher was; Tang replied: "I serve Huan Rong of the Pei state."
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu casually asked He Tang who his first master had been. Tang answered, "I studied under Huan Rong of Pei."

sentences.zh: 」帝即召榮，令說尚書，甚善之。

translations.literal: " The emperor immediately summoned Rong, ordered him to expound the Documents, and greatly approved of him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor summoned Huan Rong at once, had him lecture on the Documents, and was deeply impressed.

sentences.zh: [二]拜為議郎，賜錢十萬，入使授太子。

translations.literal: [Two] He was appointed gentleman consultant, granted one hundred thousand cash, and entered to be sent to instruct the heir.
translations.idiomatic: He was named a gentleman consultant, given a hundred thousand cash, and brought into the palace to teach the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 每朝會，輒令榮於公卿前敷奏經書。

translations.literal: At each court assembly he always ordered Rong before the dukes and ministers to spread and present the classics.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the court met, the emperor had Huan Rong expound a passage from the classics before the high ministers.

sentences.zh: 帝稱善，曰：「得生幾晚！

translations.literal: The emperor praised it as good, saying: "Having obtained this scholar, how late it is!"
translations.idiomatic: Delighted, the emperor exclaimed, "Why did I only find this man so late in the day?"

sentences.zh: 」會歐陽博士缺，帝欲用榮。

translations.literal: " It happened that the Ouyang erudite post was vacant; the emperor wished to use Rong.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward the chair for the Ouyang Book of Documents fell vacant, and the emperor meant to give it to Huan Rong.

sentences.zh: 榮叩頭讓曰：「臣經術淺薄，不如同門生郎中彭閎、揚州從事戲弘。」

translations.literal: Rong knocked his head and declined, saying: "Your servant's classical learning is shallow and thin; I am not equal to fellow students Gentlemen of the Household Peng Hong and clerk Xi Hong of Yang province."
translations.idiomatic: Huan Rong kowtowed and demurred: "My grasp of the classics is shallow; I am not the equal of my classmates Peng Hong, a gentleman of the household, and Xi Hong, a provincial clerk in Yangzhou."

sentences.zh: 帝曰：「俞，往，女諧。

translations.literal: The emperor said: "Yu, go; you, harmonize."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Very well—go, and see that all is settled harmoniously."

sentences.zh: 」[三]因拜榮為博士，引閎、弘為議郎。

translations.literal: " [Three] Thereupon he appointed Rong erudite and drew in Peng Hong and Xi Hong as gentlemen consultants.
translations.idiomatic: With that he named Huan Rong court scholar and appointed Peng Hong and Xi Hong gentlemen consultants as well.

sentences.zh: 注[一]從音七容反。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] cong is read with the fanqie qie-rong.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The character cong ("calmly") is glossed with the fanqie spelling qi-rong.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謝承書曰：「何湯字仲弓，豫章南昌人也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] Xie Cheng's book states: "He Tang, courtesy name Zhonggong, was a man of Nanchang in Yuzhang."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Xie Cheng's history identifies He Tang, courtesy name Zhonggong, as a native of Nanchang in Yuzhang.

sentences.zh: 榮門徒常四百餘人，湯為高第，以才明知名。

translations.literal: Rong's gate disciples were constantly over four hundred; Tang was of high rank and by talent and clarity was known.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Rong's lecture hall regularly held more than four hundred students; He Tang stood at the top of the class and was celebrated for his brilliance.

sentences.zh: 榮年四十無子，湯乃去榮妻為更娶，生三子，榮甚重之。

translations.literal: Rong at forty had no son; Tang then removed Rong's wife and for him took another wife; she bore three sons, and Rong greatly esteemed him.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Rong had reached forty without an heir, so He Tang sent away Huan's wife and found him a new one, who bore three sons. Huan Rong ever after held him in the highest regard.

sentences.zh: 後拜郎中，守開陽門候。

translations.literal: Later he was appointed gentleman of the palace and guarded as gate warden the Kaiyang Gate.
translations.idiomatic: He Tang was later promoted to gentleman of the palace and assigned as warden of Luoyang's Kaiyang Gate.

sentences.zh: 上微行夜還，湯閉門不納，更從中東門入。

translations.literal: The sovereign went incognito and returned at night; Tang closed the gate and would not admit him; he changed and entered through the Central-East Gate.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor once returned from an incognito outing after dark; He Tang shut the gate and refused him entry, forcing the party to use the central-east gate instead.

sentences.zh: 明旦，召詣太官賜食，諸門候皆奪俸。

translations.literal: At dawn next day he summoned him to the Grand Provisioner and granted food; all the gate wardens had their salaries stripped.
translations.idiomatic: The next morning he summoned He Tang to the imperial kitchen for a royal meal, while every other gate warden lost his pay.

sentences.zh: 建武十八年夏旱，公卿皆暴露請雨。

translations.literal: In the eighteenth year of Jianwu, summer drought, dukes and ministers all exposed themselves in supplication for rain.
translations.idiomatic: In the summer drought of 42 CE the senior ministers stood in the open sun, praying for rain.

sentences.zh: 洛陽令著車蓋出門，湯將□士鉤令車收案，有詔免令官，拜湯虎賁中郎將。

translations.literal: The Luoyang magistrate, wearing a carriage canopy, went out the gate; Tang led county clerks, hooked the magistrate's cart, seized and investigated; there was an edict removing the magistrate from office and appointing Tang Gentlemen-of-the-Household Rapid as Tigers.
translations.idiomatic: The magistrate of Luoyang rode out under a parasol while the capital prayed for rain. He Tang led county officers, seized the cart by hook, and had the case pressed. An edict dismissed the magistrate and promoted He Tang to captain of the Rapid-as-Tigers guard.

sentences.zh: 上澗歎曰：『赳赳武夫，公侯干城，何湯之謂也。

translations.literal: The sovereign sighed and said: "The stalwart warrior, bulwark of lord and marquis—this speaks of He Tang."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sighed, "A stalwart bulwark for ruler and realm—that ode might have been composed for He Tang."

sentences.zh: 』湯以明經嘗授太子，推薦榮，榮拜五更，封關內侯。

translations.literal: " Tang by clarity in the classics had once instructed the heir; he recommended Rong; Rong was appointed Elder of Fivefold Renewal and enfeoffed marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: He Tang, who had taught the crown prince the classics, now recommended Huan Rong, who was named an elder of the five ranks and enfeoffed as a marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 榮常言曰：『此皆何仲弓之力也。

translations.literal: Rong often said: "This is all the power of He Zhonggong."
translations.idiomatic: Huan Rong used to say, "Everything I have I owe to He Zhonggong."

sentences.zh: 』」注[三]續漢書曰：「閎字作明。

translations.literal: Closing the quotation from Xie Cheng's text. Commentary [three] the Continued Book of Han states: "Hong's courtesy name is written Ming."
translations.idiomatic: End of the passage quoted from Xie Cheng. Note 3: The Continued Book of Han records Peng Hong's courtesy name as Ming.

sentences.zh: 」俞，然也。

translations.literal: " Yu means yes.
translations.idiomatic: Yu means "yes," assent.

sentences.zh: 然其所舉，□令往，言汝能和諧此官。

translations.literal: Yet as for what he recommended, ordered to go, it says you can harmonize this office.
translations.idiomatic: As for those he recommended, the emperor sent them forward with orders to work in harmony with the new post.

sentences.zh: 謝承書曰：「戲弘字奉卿，吳郡人也。

translations.literal: Xie Cheng's book states: "Xi Hong, courtesy name Fengqing, was a man of Wu commandery."
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng's history describes Xi Hong, courtesy name Fengqing, as a man of Wu commandery.

sentences.zh: 家代為冠族。

translations.literal: His family for generations was a cap-clan.
translations.idiomatic: His family had been a leading clan for generations.

sentences.zh: 少有英才，與桓榮相善。

translations.literal: In youth he had outstanding talent and was on good terms with Huan Rong.
translations.idiomatic: Gifted from youth, he became a close friend of Huan Rong.

sentences.zh: 子徽，至司徒長史」也。

translations.literal: His son Hui reached grand tutor chief clerk" also.
translations.idiomatic: His son Xi Hui rose to chief clerk to the minister of education."

sentences.zh: 車駕幸大學，會諸博士論難於前，榮被服儒衣，溫恭有蘊籍，[一]辯明經義，每以禮讓相猒，不以辭長勝人，儒者莫之及，[二]特加賞賜。

translations.literal: The imperial carriage visited the Great Academy; assembling the erudites, they disputed difficulties before him; Rong wore Confucian robes, was warm and respectful and had accumulated reserve; [one] he debated and clarified classic meanings, always with ritual yielding mutually yielding, not using words' length to overcome others; Confucians none matched him; [two] he was specially increased in rewards and grants.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor visited the Imperial Academy and the scholars debated before the throne, Huan Rong appeared in full scholar's dress, gentle, deferential, and composed. He clarified the classics with courtesy rather than verbal one-upmanship, and none of the other ru could match him. The court singled him out for lavish gifts.

sentences.zh: 又詔諸生雅吹擊磬，盡日乃罷。

translations.literal: Again he edicted the students to ya-blow and strike chimes; only when the day was exhausted did they stop.
translations.idiomatic: He also ordered the students to play the stone chimes in the proper ya mode, and the music lasted the whole day.

sentences.zh: [三]後榮入會庭中，詔賜奇果，受者皆懷之，榮獨舉手捧之以拜。

translations.literal: [Three] Later Rong entered and met in the courtyard; an edict granted rare fruits; those who received them all hid them in their bosom; Rong alone raised both hands, held them, and bowed with them.
translations.idiomatic: Later, when Huan Rong attended a garden audience, the emperor sent out rare fruit. Everyone else tucked the gift away; Huan Rong alone lifted his with both hands and bowed over them in thanks.

sentences.zh: 帝笑指之曰：「此真儒生也。

translations.literal: The emperor laughed, pointed at him, and said: "This is a true Confucian scholar."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor laughed, pointed, and said, "There stands a real Confucian."

sentences.zh: 」以是愈見敬厚，常令止宿太子宮。

translations.literal: " On this account he was increasingly seen as respected and thickly favored; he was always ordered to lodge in the heir's palace.
translations.idiomatic: From then on he was held in ever deeper esteem and was regularly told to spend the night in the crown prince's residence.

sentences.zh: 積五年，榮薦門下生九江胡憲侍講，乃聽得出，旦一入而已。

translations.literal: Accumulating five years, Rong recommended his gate student Hu Xian of Jiujiang as attendant expositor, and only then was he permitted to go out, entering only once at dawn.
translations.idiomatic: After five years Huan Rong recommended his pupil Hu Xian of Jiujiang as co-tutor, and only then was he allowed to leave the palace each evening, returning once at dawn.

sentences.zh: 榮嘗寢病，太子朝夕遣中傅問病，賜以珍羞、帷帳、奴婢，謂曰：「如有不諱，無憂家室也。

translations.literal: Rong once lay ill in bed; the heir morning and evening sent the palace tutor to inquire of the illness, granted rare delicacies, curtains, bed-awning, and slaves and maids, saying to him: "If there should be the unmentionable, do not worry about household and chamber."
translations.idiomatic: When Huan Rong fell gravely ill, the crown prince sent his tutor morning and night with gifts of fine food, hangings, and servants, and told him, "If the worst should happen, your family will want for nothing."

sentences.zh: 」[四]後病癒，復入侍講。

translations.literal: " [Four] Later the illness was cured; he again entered to attend exposition.
translations.idiomatic: When he recovered, he returned to the palace to resume his teaching.

sentences.zh: 注[一]蘊籍猶言寬博有餘也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] yunji is like saying broad and ample with surplus.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Yunji here means breadth of manner, an easy, ample reserve.

sentences.zh: 蘊音於問反。

translations.literal: Yun is read with the fanqie yu-wen.
translations.idiomatic: The character yun is glossed yu-wen in the fanqie spelling.

sentences.zh: 注[二]猒，服也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] yan means submit.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Here yan means to defer or yield to one another.

sentences.zh: 音一葉反。

translations.literal: Sound one-ye fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: It is read with the fanqie yi-ye.

sentences.zh: 注[三]吹管奏雅頌也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] blowing pipes performs ya and eulogia.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: The students were playing wind instruments in the ya and song modes.

sentences.zh: 注[四]不諱謂死也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four] buhui means death.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: "The unmentionable" is a euphemism for death.

sentences.zh: 死者人之常，故言不諱也。

translations.literal: Death is man's constant; therefore it is called the unmentionable.
translations.idiomatic: Because dying is the common lot of humanity, polite speech calls it "what cannot be spoken of."

sentences.zh: 二十八年，大會百官，詔問誰可傅太子者，腢臣承望上意，皆言太子舅執金吾原鹿侯陰識可。

translations.literal: In the twenty-eighth year, a great assembly of hundred officials; an edict asked who could tutor the heir; coupling ministers received and looked to the superior's intent, all saying the heir's maternal uncle, Bearer of the Mace, Marquis of Yuanlu Yin Shi, was suitable.
translations.idiomatic: In 52 CE the emperor convened his officials and asked who should tutor the crown prince. Reading his mood, the ministers all named the prince's uncle Yin Shi, marquis of Yuanlu and Bearer of the Mace.

sentences.zh: [一]博士張佚正色曰：「今陛下立太子，為陰氏乎？

translations.literal: [One] Erudite Zhang Yi with correct color said: "Now Your Majesty establishes an heir—for the Yin clan?"
translations.idiomatic: The scholar Zhang Yi spoke up sternly: "Is Your Majesty installing an heir for the house of Yin,"

sentences.zh: 為天下乎？

translations.literal: For all under heaven?"
translations.idiomatic: or for the empire?"

sentences.zh: 即為陰氏，則陰侯可；

translations.literal: If it is for the Yin clan, then the Yin marquis is acceptable;
translations.idiomatic: If it is for the Yins, then Lord Yin will do;

sentences.zh: 為天下，則固宜用天下之賢才。

translations.literal: if it is for all under heaven, then solidly one ought to use all under heaven's worthy talents.
translations.idiomatic: if it is for the realm, you must choose the best talent in the realm."

sentences.zh: 」帝稱善，曰：「欲置傅者，以輔太子也。

translations.literal: " The emperor praised it as good, saying: "Desiring to appoint a tutor is to assist the heir."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved. "A tutor is meant to guide the heir,"

sentences.zh: 今博士不難正朕，況太子乎？

translations.literal: Now an erudite does not shrink from correcting me—how much more the heir?"
translations.idiomatic: he said. "If a court scholar will correct me to my face, what better model could the crown prince have?"

sentences.zh: 」即拜佚為太子太傅，而以榮為少傅，賜以輜車、乘馬。

translations.literal: " He immediately appointed Yi as heir grand tutor and made Rong junior tutor, granting covered carriage and riding horses.
translations.idiomatic: He thereupon named Zhang Yi grand tutor to the heir apparent and Huan Rong junior tutor, and presented them with covered carriages and teams.

sentences.zh: 榮大會諸生，陳其車馬、印綬，曰：「今日所蒙，稽古之力也，可不勉哉！

translations.literal: Rong greatly assembled the students, displayed his carriage horses and seal ribbons, saying: "What today we receive is the power of investigating antiquity—how can we not exert ourselves!"
translations.idiomatic: Huan Rong gathered his students, displayed the imperial carriage, horses, and seals, and declared, "Today's honors come from devotion to the classics—how can we fail to press on?"

sentences.zh: 」榮以太子經學成畢，上疏謝曰：「臣幸得侍帷幄，執經連年，而智學淺短，無以補益萬分。

translations.literal: " Rong because the heir's classic learning was completed, submitted a memorial thanking, saying: "Your servant fortunately obtained to serve within the tent curtain, holding the classics successive years, yet wisdom-learning is shallow and short; there is nothing wherewith to benefit the myriad part."
translations.idiomatic: When the crown prince had finished his classical course, Huan Rong submitted a memorial of thanks: "I have been privileged to attend you in the inner quarters and hold the classics year after year, yet my learning remains slight and has scarcely repaid your favor."

sentences.zh: 今皇太子以聰叡之姿，通明經義，觀覽古今，儲君副主莫能專精博學若此者也。

translations.literal: Now the heir apparent by intelligent and penetrating posture penetrates clarifies classic meanings, observes and peruses ancient and modern; heir-apparent deputy lord none can specialize essence and broadly learn like this one.
translations.idiomatic: The heir apparent's native intelligence has opened the meaning of the classics to him; he ranges across past and present as no other heir in memory has done through sheer breadth of study."

sentences.zh: 斯誠國家福佑，天下幸甚。

translations.literal: This truly is the state's blessing and aid; all under heaven is greatly fortunate.
translations.idiomatic: That is true good fortune for the dynasty and a blessing for the whole realm."

sentences.zh: 臣師道已盡，皆在太子，謹使掾臣汜再拜歸道。

translations.literal: Your servant's teacher-way is already exhausted; all is in the heir; respectfully your servant causes clerk Fan twice to bow and return the way.
translations.idiomatic: I have taught all I know; it now rests with the heir. I therefore send my clerk Fan Si to bow twice and convey my withdrawal from instruction.

sentences.zh: 」[二]太子報書曰：「莊以童蒙，學道九載，而典訓不明，無所曉識。

translations.literal: " [Two] The heir returned a letter saying: "Zhuang by child obscurity, learning the way nine years, yet canonical instruction is not clear; there is nothing understood and known."
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince replied: "Zhuang began as a child ignorant of letters; for nine years I have studied the Way, yet the norms of the classics remain dim to me, and I still understand little."

sentences.zh: 夫五經廣大，聖言幽遠，非天下之至精，豈能與於此！

translations.literal: The five classics are vast and great; sage words are dark and far; if not all under heaven's utmost essence, how can one participate in this!"
translations.idiomatic: The Five Classics are vast and the sages' words abstruse; without the keenest mind alive, who could master them?"

sentences.zh: [三]況以不才，敢承誨命。

translations.literal: [Three] Moreover with lack of talent, I dare receive instructing charge.
translations.idiomatic: As for me, lacking ability, how could I presume to refuse the lesson you offer?"

sentences.zh: 昔之先師謝弟子者有矣，上則通達經旨，分明章句，[四]下則去家慕鄉，求謝師門。

translations.literal: Of old there have been cases of a first teacher taking leave of a disciple: in the high case penetrating the classics' gist and clarifying chapter and sentence; [four] in the low case leaving home and yearning for native place, seeking to bid farewell at the teacher's gate.
translations.idiomatic: History records masters who released a finished pupil: the finest left having mastered the text and every subdivision; [four] the humblest went home lovesick for kin, yet still returned to the master's door to give thanks."

sentences.zh: [五] 今蒙下列，不敢有辭，願君慎疾加餐，重愛玉體。

translations.literal: [Five] Now that I am listed among those released, I dare not decline; I wish you guard against illness, take more food, and greatly cherish your jade body."
translations.idiomatic: [Five] Now that you have granted me leave to step down, I dare not object. I pray you, sir, guard your health, take your meals, and cherish your person."

sentences.zh: 」[六] 注[一]言可任也。

translations.literal: " [Six] Commentary [one] the words mean can be entrusted.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The earlier remark means that Yin Shi was considered a suitable appointment.

sentences.zh: 注[二]續漢書曰：「三公東西曹掾四百石，余掾比二百石。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] the Continued Book of Han states: "The three excellencies' east and west bureau clerks are four hundred bushels; remaining clerks are comparable two hundred bushels."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The Continued Book of Han states that clerks of the eastern and western bureaus under the Three Excellencies ranked at four hundred bushels and the rest at two hundred.

sentences.zh: 」歸猶謝也。

translations.literal: " Gui is like xie (thank)."
translations.idiomatic: Here gui means to decline or take leave, the same as xie.

sentences.zh: 注[三]此上二句，周易之系辭。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] these above two sentences are the Changes of Zhou's appended phrases.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: The two lines just quoted come from the Appended Remarks to the Zhouyi.

sentences.zh: 與音預。

translations.literal: Yu is read yu (participate).
translations.idiomatic: Yu ("participate") is read like the word yu meaning "to take part in."

sentences.zh: 注[四]前書丁寬受學於田何，學成，何謝寬，寬東歸，何謂門人曰：「易東矣。」

translations.literal: Commentary [four] the former book: Ding Kuan received learning from Tian He; learning completed, He thanked Kuan; Kuan east returned; He said to gate men: "The Changes have gone east.""
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: The earlier history tells how Ding Kuan finished his study under Tian He; the master then dismissed his pupil, Ding returned east, and Tian He told his followers, "The Book of Changes is traveling eastward."

sentences.zh: 是先師謝弟子。

translations.literal: This is the former teacher thanking the disciple.
translations.idiomatic: That is an instance of the master dismissing the disciple.

sentences.zh: 注[五]韓詩外傳曰「孔子行，見戲魚哭。

translations.literal: Commentary [five] Han Shi Wai Zhuan states: "Confucius was traveling and saw Gao Yu weeping" (the text writes 戲魚 for 皋魚).
translations.idiomatic: Note 5: The Han Shi Wai Zhuan tells how Confucius, on the road, met Gao Yu in tears—the name is written with variant graphs in some manuscripts.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『子非有喪，何哭悲也？

translations.literal: Confucius said: "You do not have mourning; why weep so sadly?"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius asked, "You are not in mourning—why weep as if you were?"

sentences.zh: 』戲魚曰：『吾少而好學，周流諸侯，以沒吾親。

translations.literal: " Gao Yu said: "I in youth loved learning, circulated among the feudal lords, and so failed to be with my parents when they died."
translations.idiomatic: Gao Yu replied, "I loved books in youth and wandered the states in their service, and my parents died before I could support them."

sentences.zh: 樹欲靜而風不止，子欲養而親不待。

translations.literal: The tree desires stillness but wind does not stop; the son desires to nurture but kin do not wait.
translations.idiomatic: The tree would be still, yet the wind never ceases; the child would repay his parents, yet they are gone too soon."

sentences.zh: 往而不可追者年也，去而不見者親也。

translations.literal: What goes and cannot be pursued is years; what departs and is not seen is parents.
translations.idiomatic: The years that pass cannot be overtaken; the parents who depart cannot be seen again."

sentences.zh: 』孔子曰：『弟子識之。

translations.literal: " Confucius said: "Disciples, mark this well."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius told his disciples, "Burn this lesson into your memory."

sentences.zh: 』於是門人辭歸者十有三」也。

translations.literal: " Thereupon of gate men who took leave and returned there were thirteen" also.
translations.idiomatic: Thirteen disciples thereupon resigned and went home to their families."

sentences.zh: 注[六]史記曰：「伏聞太后玉體不安。

translations.literal: Commentary [six] the Grand Scribe's Records states: "I prostrate-hear the empress dowager's jade body is not at peace."
translations.idiomatic: Note 6: The Shiji quotes a memorial opening, "I have heard with bowed head that Her Majesty's precious health is unsteady."

sentences.zh: 」君子於玉比德，故以言也。

translations.literal: " The gentleman toward jade compares virtue; therefore by it he speaks."
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman likens virtue to fine jade, hence the polite reference to the "jade body."

sentences.zh: 三十年，拜為太常。

translations.literal: In the thirtieth year, he was appointed grand regular.
translations.idiomatic: In 54 CE he was appointed minister of rituals.

sentences.zh: 榮初遭倉卒，與族人桓元卿同饑□，而榮講誦不息。

translations.literal: Rong at first encountered haste; with clansman Huan Yuanqing together hungry and distressed, yet Rong lecturing and reciting did not cease.
translations.idiomatic: In the first panic of the wars Huan Rong and his kinsman Huan Yuanqing shivered and starved together, yet Huan Rong never stopped chanting the classics.

sentences.zh: 元卿嗤榮曰：「但自苦氣力，何時復施用乎？

translations.literal: Yuanqing sneered at Rong, saying: "Only yourself suffering energy strength—when again will it be applied and used?"
translations.idiomatic: Yuanqing mocked him: "You only wear yourself out—when will any of this ever matter?"

sentences.zh: 」榮笑不應。

translations.literal: " Rong laughed and did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Rong smiled and said nothing.

sentences.zh: 及為太常，元卿歎曰：「我農家子，豈意學之為利乃若是哉！

translations.literal: When he became grand regular, Yuanqing sighed, saying: "I am a farmer's son—how could I have thought study's being profit would be thus!"
translations.idiomatic: When Huan Rong became minister of rituals, Yuanqing sighed, "I was born a farmer's son; I never dreamed scholarship could bring a man this far."

sentences.zh: 」[一] 注[一]東觀漢記曰：「榮為太常，元卿來候榮，榮諸弟子謂曰：『平生笑盡氣力，今何如？

translations.literal: " [One] Commentary [one] Eastern Lodge Han Record states: "When Rong was grand regular, Yuanqing came to visit Rong; Rong's various disciples said to him: "In life you laughed exhausting strength—now how is it?"
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Eastern Lodge Record says that when Huan Rong took office, Yuanqing came to call. The students teased him, "You used to ridicule him for wasting his strength—what do you say now?"

sentences.zh: 』元卿曰：『我安能知此哉！

translations.literal: " Yuanqing said: "How could I know this!"
translations.idiomatic: Yuanqing replied, "How was I to know it would come to this?"

sentences.zh: 』」顯宗即位，尊以師禮，甚見親重，拜二子為郎。

translations.literal: "" When the Manifest Ancestor ascended, he honored with teacher rites, was greatly seen as intimate and heavy, appointed two sons as gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Ming came to the throne he treated Huan Rong with full master's rites, held him in the closest confidence, and appointed two of his sons gentlemen of the palace.

sentences.zh: 榮年踰八十，自以衰老，數上書乞身，輒加賞賜。

translations.literal: Rong's years crossed eighty; he himself thought himself old and declining, repeatedly submitted memorials begging his person, and each time was increased in rewards and grants.
translations.idiomatic: Past eighty, Huan Rong judged himself too frail for office and repeatedly asked to retire; each time the court answered with fresh honors and gifts.

sentences.zh: 乘輿嘗幸太常府，令榮坐東面，設幾杖，會百官驃騎將軍東平王蒼以下及榮門生數百人，天子親自執業，每言輒曰「大師在是」。

translations.literal: The imperial carriage once visited the Grand Regular's office, ordered Rong to sit facing east, set out table and staff, assembled the hundred officials from the general-in-chief of agile cavalry, the king of Dongping, Cang, downward, and Rong's gate students several hundred persons; the Son of Heaven personally held the learning task, and whenever he spoke always said "The great master is here."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor once paid a call on the minister of rituals' residence, had Huan Rong seated with his back to the east—the seat of honor—supplied him with a desk and cane, and gathered the high ministers from the general of swift cavalry and Prince Cang of Dongping down to several hundred of Huan's own students. The sovereign himself took the pupil's part, and whenever he spoke he would say only, "The great master is present."

sentences.zh: [一]既罷，悉以太官供具賜太常家。

translations.literal: [One] When finished, all the Grand Provisioner's supplies were granted to the Grand Regular's household.
translations.idiomatic: [1] When the visit ended he had the entire imperial banquet service sent to Huan Rong's home.

sentences.zh: 其恩禮若此。

translations.literal: His grace and ritual were like this.
translations.idiomatic: Such was the favor shown him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]東觀記曰「時執經生避位發難，上謙曰『大師在是』」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] the Eastern Lodge Record states: "At that time the classic-holding students yielded place and raised difficulties; the sovereign modestly said 'The great master is here.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Eastern Lodge Record says that when students holding the classics yielded their seats to press hard questions, the emperor modestly replied, "The great master is here."

sentences.zh: 永平二年，三雍初成，拜榮為五更。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongping, the three Yong were first completed; Rong was appointed Elder of Fivefold Renewal.
translations.idiomatic: In 59 CE, when the three ritual complexes—the Bright Hall, the Spirit Terrace, and the Ring Moat Academy—were finished, Huan Rong was named elder of the fifth order.

sentences.zh: [一]每大射養老禮畢，帝輒引榮及弟子升堂，執經自為下說。

translations.literal: [One] Whenever the great archery and nurture-the-aged ritual was completed, the emperor always drew Rong and disciples ascending the hall, holding the classics himself and for himself speaking downward explanation.
translations.idiomatic: [1] After each grand archery ceremony and banquet for the aged, the emperor would lead Huan Rong and his students up into the hall, take the text in his own hands, and play disciple while Huan expounded from below.

sentences.zh: [二]乃封榮為關內侯，食邑五千戶。

translations.literal: [Two] Thereupon he enfeoffed Rong as marquis within the passes, food estate five thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He then enfeoffed Huan Rong as marquis within the passes with a fief of five thousand households.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]三雍，宮也，謂明堂、靈台、辟雍。

translations.literal: [Three] Commentary [one] the three Yong are palaces, meaning the Bright Hall, Spirit Terrace, and Ring Moat Academy.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Note 1: The "three Yong" are ritual buildings: the Bright Hall, the Spirit Terrace, and the imperial academy in its moated ring.

sentences.zh: 前書音義曰：「皆□天人雍和之氣為之，故謂三雍。

translations.literal: The Former Han commentary on pronunciation and meaning states: "All are made from the harmonious qi of the Yong rites linking Heaven and man; hence the collective name 'three Yong.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Former Han sound gloss explains that the three complexes embody the harmonious qi of the Yong sacrifice between Heaven and man, which is why they are grouped as the "three Yong."

sentences.zh: 」五更，解見明紀。

translations.literal: " Fivefold Renewal, explanation see the Bright annals.
translations.idiomatic: On the title "elder of the fifth order," see the annals of Emperor Ming.

sentences.zh: 注[二]下說謂下語而講說之也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] xia shuo means speaking downward words and expounding them.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: "Speaking from below" means the emperor took the lower place and listened while the teacher lectured.

sentences.zh: 注[三]東觀記曰：「榮以尚書授朕十有餘年。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] the Eastern Lodge Record states: "Rong by the Documents instructed Us for more than ten years."
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: The Eastern Lodge Record quotes the emperor: "Huan Rong has taught Us the Documents for more than a decade."

sentences.zh: 詩云：『日就月將，示我顯德行。

translations.literal: The Odes say: "Day by day, month by month, show me bright virtue in conduct."
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry says, "Advancing day by day and month by month, you display illustrious virtue."

sentences.zh: 』乃封之。」

translations.literal: " Thereupon he enfeoffed him."
translations.idiomatic: So I granted him a fief."

sentences.zh: 榮每疾病，帝輒遣使者存問，太官、太醫相望於道。

translations.literal: Whenever Rong had illness, the emperor always sent envoys to inquire after him; Grand Provisioner and Grand Physician followed one another on the road.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Huan Rong fell ill, the emperor dispatched messengers to ask after him, and imperial physicians and kitchen stewards crowded the road to his door.

sentences.zh: 及篤，上疏謝恩，讓還爵土。

translations.literal: When critically ill, he submitted a memorial thanking grace, yielding return of title and land.
translations.idiomatic: When his condition turned grave he memorialized his gratitude and asked to surrender his title and fief.

sentences.zh: 帝幸其家問起居，入街下車，擁經而前，撫榮垂涕，賜以默茵、帷帳、刀□、衣被，良久乃去。

translations.literal: The emperor visited his house asking rise and rest, entered the lane dismounted carriage, embraced the classics and went forward, stroked Rong and shed tears, granted him mo mat, curtains, bed-awning, knife and belt hook, clothes and coverlets, long time then departed.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor called at his home to inquire after him, left his chariot in the lane, and advanced clutching the classics to Huan Rong's bedside. He stroked the old man and wept, then presented him with a dark cushion, hangings, weapons, and bedding, and stayed a long while before he left.

sentences.zh: 自是諸侯將軍大夫問疾者，不敢復乘車到門，皆拜默下。

translations.literal: From this, marquises, generals, and grandees who inquired after illness did not dare again ride carriage to the gate; all bowed below the mo mat.
translations.idiomatic: After that, no marquis, general, or senior official who came to ask after his health would drive a carriage to the door; each dismounted and bowed from the lane below the mat the emperor had blessed.

sentences.zh: 榮卒，帝親自變服，臨喪送葬，賜頤塋於首山之陽。

translations.literal: When Rong died, the emperor personally changed garments, attended the mourning and sent off the burial, granted a tomb mound on the south of Mount Shou.
translations.idiomatic: When Huan Rong died, the emperor changed out of court dress, attended the funeral in person, and granted him a burial mound on the southern slope of Mount Shou.

sentences.zh: [一]除兄子二人補四百石，都講生八人補二百石，其餘門徒多至公卿。

translations.literal: [One] He appointed two elder brothers' sons to supplement four hundred bushels, eight chief-lecturer students to supplement two hundred bushels; the remaining gate disciples many reached dukes and ministers.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Two of his nephews were given posts at four hundred bushels, eight senior lecture students at two hundred bushels, and many of his other disciples rose to the highest offices.

sentences.zh: [二]子郁嗣。

translations.literal: [Two] His son Yu succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: [2] His son Huan Yu inherited the title.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]首陽山在今偃師縣西北也。

translations.literal: [Three] Commentary [one] Mount Shouyang is now northwest of Yanshi county.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Note 1: Mount Shouyang lies northwest of present-day Yanshi county.

sentences.zh: 注[二]華嶠書曰：「榮弟子丁鴻學最高。」

translations.literal: Commentary [two] Hua Qiao's book states: "Among Rong's disciples Ding Hong's learning was highest."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Hua Qiao's history names Ding Hong as the foremost of Huan Rong's students in scholarship.

sentences.zh: 注[三]華嶠書曰：「榮長子雍早卒，少子郁嗣。」

translations.literal: Commentary [three] Hua Qiao's book states: "Rong's eldest son Yong died early; the younger son Yu succeeded."
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Hua Qiao records that Huan Rong's eldest son Yong died young and the younger son Yu carried on the line.

sentences.zh: 論曰：張佚訐切陰侯，以取高位，危言犯觿，義動明後，知其直有餘也。

translations.literal: The appraisal states: Zhang Yi denounced and cut the Yin marquis to obtain high position; perilous words offended the heir's belt-hook; righteousness moved the bright later ruler—one knows his straightness had surplus.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: Zhang Yi won high office by bluntly challenging the Yin clan's monopoly on the tutorship; his daring words caught even the heir's attention and stirred a wise ruler—here was integrity in full measure.

sentences.zh: 若夫一言納賞，志士為之懷恥；

translations.literal: As for one word receiving reward, a determined scholar for it harbors shame;
translations.idiomatic: Yet to win a rich reward on the strength of a single remark is something a man of principle would find shameful;

sentences.zh: [一]受爵不讓，風人所以興歌。

translations.literal: [One] receiving a noble rank without declining is why the Airs poet therefore raised song.
translations.idiomatic: [1] and to accept a fief without demur is what moved the poets of the Airs to compose their verse.

sentences.zh: [二]而佚廷議戚援，自居全德，[三]意者以廉不足乎？

translations.literal: [Two] Yet Yi in court debated the kin's support, himself occupying complete virtue, [three] does the intention mean that in incorruptibility there was insufficiency?
translations.idiomatic: [2] Still, at court Zhang Yi had argued for talent over nepotism, then stepped forward to take the grand tutor's post himself—[3] was he, after all, less than perfect in unstained conduct?

sentences.zh: 昔樂羊食子，有功見疑；

translations.literal: Formerly Yue Yang ate his son, had merit yet was suspected;
translations.idiomatic: Yue Yang drank soup made from his own son's flesh and was distrusted even after his victory;

sentences.zh: 西巴放麑，以罪作傅。

translations.literal: Xi Ba released the fawn, by crime became tutor.
translations.idiomatic: Xi Ba set a captured fawn free and was punished, yet was later made tutor to the heir.

sentences.zh: [四]蓋推仁審偽，本乎其情。

translations.literal: [Four] For extending benevolence and examining falsehood, the root lies in their qing.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Judging true benevolence against mere pretense always comes back to the heart's motive.

sentences.zh: 君人者能以此察，則真邪幾於辨矣。

translations.literal: If the lord of men can by this observe, then true and deviant are nearly distinguished.
translations.idiomatic: A ruler who tests men in this way will come close to telling loyalty from sham.

sentences.zh: [五] 注[一]秦兵圍趙，時魯仲連在趙，因說令退兵。

translations.literal: [Five] Commentary [one] Qin troops besieged Zhao; at that time Lu Zhonglian was in Zhao, thereby persuaded them to order withdrawal of troops.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Note 1: When Qin besieged Zhao, Lu Zhonglian was in Handan and talked the besiegers into lifting the siege.

sentences.zh: 平原君趙勝乃以千金為仲連壽，連笑曰：「所貴於天下之士者，能排患解紛而無取也。

translations.literal: Lord Pingyuan Zhao Sheng then took a thousand pounds of gold as Zhonglian's longevity gift; Lian laughed and said: "What is prized in knights under heaven is the ability to dispel disaster and unravel strife without taking reward."
translations.idiomatic: Lord Pingyuan offered Lu Zhonglian a thousand in gold as a birthday gift; Lu laughed and said, "The true knight relieves calamity and untangles strife without pocketing a fee."

sentences.zh: 即有取者，是商賈之事也，而連不忍為也。

translations.literal: If there is one who takes, that is the affair of merchants, and Lian cannot bear to do it.
translations.idiomatic: To take payment would be huckster's work, and I will not stoop to it."

sentences.zh: 」遂去，終身不復見。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he departed, to the end of life not again seeing.
translations.idiomatic: With that he left and never saw Lord Pingyuan again.

sentences.zh: 見史記也。

translations.literal: See the Grand Scribe's Records.
translations.idiomatic: The full story is in the Records of the Grand Historian.

sentences.zh: 注[二]詩小雅角弓篇曰：「受爵不讓，至於己斯亡。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] the Odes minor ya Horn Bow piece states: "Receiving rank without yielding, as to oneself thus perishes."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The Horn Bow ode in the Minor Odes runs, "He takes rank without yielding—thus he brings ruin on himself."

sentences.zh: 」風人猶詩人也。

translations.literal: " The Airs man is like the Odes man.
translations.idiomatic: "The poets of the Airs" simply means the poets of the Classic of Poetry.

sentences.zh: 注[三]佚諫云「當用天下之賢才」，而乃自當其任，故曰「自居全德」。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] Yi's remonstrance said "one ought to use all under heaven's worthy talents," yet then himself took that duty; therefore it says "himself occupying complete virtue."
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Zhang Yi had urged the throne to choose the worthiest man in the realm, then accepted the grand tutorship himself—hence the comment that he "claimed complete virtue for himself."

sentences.zh: 全德言無玷缺也。

translations.literal: Complete virtue speaks of no stain or lack.
translations.idiomatic: "Complete virtue" means conduct without flaw.

sentences.zh: 莊子曰「是謂全德」也。

translations.literal: Zhuangzi states "this is called complete virtue" also.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuangzi uses the same phrase of a man whose character is whole.

sentences.zh: 注[四]並解見吳漢傳。

translations.literal: Commentary [four] both explanations see Wu Han's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: The parallels of Yue Yang and Xi Ba are discussed in the biography of Wu Han.

sentences.zh: 注[五]幾，近也，音鉅依反。

translations.literal: Commentary [five] ji means near; sound ju-yi fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5: Ji here means "almost"; it is read with the fanqie ju-yi.

sentences.zh: 郁字仲恩，少以父任為郎。

translations.literal: Yu, courtesy name Zhongen, in youth by father's appointment became gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Yu, courtesy name Zhongen, entered the palace as a gentleman cadet through his father's privilege.

sentences.zh: 敦厚篤學，傳父業，以尚書教授，門徒常數百人。

translations.literal: He was generous and thick, devoted to learning, transmitted father's occupation, by the Documents instructed; gate disciples were constantly several hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: Steadfast and bookish, he carried on his father's teaching of the Book of Documents to a lecture hall that again numbered in the hundreds.

sentences.zh: 榮卒，郁當襲爵，上書讓于兄子泛，顯宗不許，不得已受封，悉以租入與之。

translations.literal: When Rong died, Yu ought to have inherited the noble rank; he submitted a memorial yielding to elder brother's son Fan; the Manifest Ancestor did not permit; unavoidably accepting the enfeoffment, he entirely gave the rent income to him.
translations.idiomatic: When Huan Rong died, Huan Yu was heir to the marquisate but memorialized asking to pass the title to his cousin Fan. Emperor Ming refused. Yu accepted the fief only under protest and handed every copper of the rental income to Fan.

sentences.zh: 帝以郁先師子，有禮讓，甚見親厚，常居中論經書，問以政事，稍遷侍中。

translations.literal: The emperor because Yu was the former master's son, had ritual yielding, was greatly seen as intimate and thick, constantly was within expounding classics, asked with government affairs, gradually moved to palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Because he was the old tutor's son and had shown such modesty, the emperor kept him close, had him discourse on the classics in the inner palace, consulted him on policy, and gradually promoted him to palace attendant.

sentences.zh: [一] 帝自製五家要說章句，令郁校定於宣明殿，[二]以侍中監虎賁中郎將。

translations.literal: [One] The emperor personally made five-house essential explanation chapter-and-sentence, ordered Yu to collate and fix it at the Xuanyang Hall, [two] as palace attendant supervised Gentlemen-of-the-Household Rapid as Tigers.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The emperor drafted his own commentary on the Five Classics in chapter-and-sentence form and had Huan Yu collate it in the Xuanyang Hall; [2] as palace attendant he also oversaw the Rapid-as-Tigers guard.

sentences.zh: 注[一]東觀記曰「永平十四年為議郎，遷侍中」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] the Eastern Lodge Record states "In the fourteenth year of Yongping became gentleman consultant, moved to palace attendant" also.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Eastern Lodge Record places his appointment as gentleman consultant in 71 CE and his promotion to palace attendant soon after.

sentences.zh: 注[二]華嶠書曰「帝自製五行章句」，此言「五家」，即謂五行之家也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] Hua Qiao's book states "The emperor personally made the Five Agents chapter-and-sentence"; this speech "five houses" means namely the Five Agents school.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Hua Qiao says the sovereign wrote a commentary on the Five Phases; "five houses" here is the same tradition.

sentences.zh: 宣明殿在德陽殿後。

translations.literal: Xuanyang Hall is behind Deyang Hall.
translations.idiomatic: The Xuanyang Hall stood behind the Deyang Hall in the palace.

sentences.zh: 東觀記曰：「上謂郁曰：『卿經及先師，致覆文雅。

translations.literal: The Eastern Lodge Record states: "The sovereign said to Yu: "Your classics reach to the former master, overturning and elegant in pattern."
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Lodge Record quotes the emperor telling Huan Yu, "Your learning matches your father's; your diction is refined and sure."

sentences.zh: 』其冬，上親於辟雍，自講所制五行章句已，復令郁說一篇。

translations.literal: " That winter, the sovereign personally at the Ring Moat Academy, after himself expounding the made Five Agents chapter-and-sentence finished, again ordered Yu to expound one piece.
translations.idiomatic: That winter, after lecturing on his own commentary on the Five Phases at the academy, he had Huan Yu present a further section.

sentences.zh: 上謂郁曰：『我為孔子，卿為子夏，起予者商也。

translations.literal: The sovereign said to Yu: "I am Confucius, you are Zixia—'It is Shang who gets me started.'"
translations.idiomatic: He said to Yu, "Let me be Confucius and you Zixia—'It is Shang who can set me on my way with the Odes.'"

sentences.zh: 』又問郁曰：『子幾人能傳學？

translations.literal: " Again asked Yu: "How many sons can transmit learning?"
translations.idiomatic: Then he asked, "How many of your sons can carry on your scholarship?"

sentences.zh: 』郁曰：『臣子皆未能傳學，孤兄子一人學方起。

translations.literal: " Yu said: "Your servant's sons all are not able to transmit learning; one orphaned elder brother's son alone learning is just rising."
translations.idiomatic: Yu answered, "None of my own boys show promise; only my late brother's orphan is beginning to take to the work."

sentences.zh: 』上曰：『努力教之，有起者即白之。

translations.literal: " The sovereign said: "Strive to teach him; when there is one who rises, then report it."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Train him hard, and tell me the moment he begins to shine."

sentences.zh: 』」永平十五年，入授皇太子經，遷越騎校尉，詔□太子、諸王各奉賀致禮。

translations.literal: "" In the fifteenth year of Yongping, he entered to instruct the heir apparent in the classics, moved to colonel of the swift cavalry; an edict ordered the crown prince and the various kings each to offer congratulations and present ritual.
translations.idiomatic: In 72 CE he entered the palace to teach the crown prince the classics, was promoted to colonel of swift cavalry, and an edict directed the heir and the princes each to send congratulations and formal gifts.

sentences.zh: 郁數進忠言，多見納錄。

translations.literal: Yu repeatedly advanced loyal words, many were received and recorded.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Yu often offered blunt counsel, and most of it was accepted.

sentences.zh: [一]肅宗即位，郁以母憂乞身，詔聽以侍中行服。

translations.literal: [One] When the Solemn Ancestor ascended, Yu by mother's mourning begged his person; an edict permitted him to observe mourning as palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: [1] When Emperor Zhang came to the throne, Huan Yu asked leave to mourn his mother; the court allowed him to keep his rank of palace attendant while in mourning.

sentences.zh: [二]建初二年，遷屯騎校尉。

translations.literal: [Two] In the second year of Jianchu, he moved to colonel of garrison cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: [2] In 77 CE he was transferred to colonel of garrison cavalry.

sentences.zh: 注[一]東觀記曰：「皇太子賜郁鞍馬、刀□，郁乃上疏皇太子曰：『伏見太子體性自然，包含今古，謙謙允恭，天下共見。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] the Eastern Lodge Record states: "The heir apparent granted Yu saddle horses, knife and belt hook; Yu then submitted a memorial to the heir apparent saying: "I prostrate-see the heir apparent's bodily nature is spontaneous, embracing ancient and modern, modestly truly respectful, all under heaven together sees."
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Eastern Lodge Record says the crown prince once gave Huan Yu horses and arms. Yu memorialized in reply: "I have observed that Your Highness's temperament is naturally balanced, comprehending past and present, humble and truly reverent, as the whole court can see."

sentences.zh: 郁父子受恩，無以明益，夙夜籩懼，誠思自竭。

translations.literal: The Yu father and son received grace, nothing wherewith to clarify benefit, morning and night anxious fear, sincerely thought to exhaust themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Your servants father and son owe a debt your kindness can never repay; we are anxious night and day and would give our last breath in your service.

sentences.zh: 愚以為太子上當合聖心，下當卓絕於觿，宜思遠慮，以光朝廷。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks the heir apparent upward ought to match the sage heart, downward ought to tower above the crowd at the belt-hook, ought to think far plans, thereby to brighten the court.
translations.idiomatic: I beg Your Highness to satisfy the sage Son of Heaven above and to stand head and shoulders above your peers at court; look to the long view and so bring luster to the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 』」注[二]華嶠書曰「郁上書乞身，天子憂之，有詔公卿議。

translations.literal: "" Commentary [two] Hua Qiao's book states "Yu submitted memorial begging his person; the Son of Heaven was troubled; there was an edict ordering dukes and ministers to deliberate.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Hua Qiao records that when Yu asked to retire for mourning, "the emperor was troubled and ordered the ministers to debate the matter."

sentences.zh: 議者皆以郁身為名儒，學者之宗，可許之，於是詔郁以侍中行服」也。

translations.literal: The deliberators all thought Yu's person was a famous Confucian, the scholars' ancestor, could permit him; thereupon an edict ordered Yu to observe mourning as palace attendant" also.
translations.idiomatic: They agreed that a scholar of his stature, the acknowledged head of the school, should be granted the request, and an edict allowed him to complete mourning without resigning his post of palace attendant."

sentences.zh: 由是遷長樂少府，復入侍講。

translations.literal: Thereby he moved to junior steward of Changle, again entered to attend exposition.
translations.idiomatic: He was then promoted to junior steward of the Changle Palace and again joined the palace lecturing staff.

sentences.zh: 頃之，轉為侍中奉車都尉。

translations.literal: Before long, he turned to palace attendant and chief commandant of chariots.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward he became palace attendant and chief commandant for the imperial carriages.

sentences.zh: 永元四年，代丁鴻為太常。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Yongyuan, he replaced Ding Hong as grand regular.
translations.idiomatic: In 92 CE he succeeded Ding Hong as minister of rituals.

sentences.zh: 明年，病卒。

translations.literal: The next year, he died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: He died of illness the following year.

sentences.zh: 注[一]自禮記以下，至此以上，皆大戴禮之文也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] from the Record of Rites downward, to this upward, all is Great Dai Ritual's text.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: From the Liji passage cited down to this point, the commentary follows the text of the Da Dai Liji.

sentences.zh: 切而不勤，謂習與智長，則常自切厲而不須勤□，若性猶自然也。

translations.literal: Cutting yet not diligent, means practice and wisdom growing, then constantly self-cutting severe and without need for external prodding, as if nature were still spontaneous.
translations.idiomatic: "Keen without being driven" means that as habit and understanding grow, a man spurs himself without external goading, almost as second nature.

sentences.zh: 襁絡也；

translations.literal: Qiang means the carrying-straps for an infant;
translations.idiomatic: Qiang denotes the bands by which an infant is carried on the back;

sentences.zh: 保，小兒被也。

translations.literal: bao, small child's quilt.
translations.idiomatic: bao is the quilt wrapped around a baby.

sentences.zh: 「保」當作「褓」，古字通也。

translations.literal: The graph written bao for "protect" should be read as bao meaning swaddling-clothes; the forms were interchangeable in antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: The character here should be understood as bao, "swaddling"; the two graphs were often interchanged in old texts.

sentences.zh: 史佚，成王時史官，名佚，賢者也。

translations.literal: Scribe Yi, King Cheng's time scribe official, named Yi, was a worthy person.
translations.idiomatic: Yi the scribe, who served under King Cheng of Zhou, was a man of noted virtue.

sentences.zh: 維，持也。

translations.literal: Wei means hold.
translations.idiomatic: Wei means to uphold or sustain.

sentences.zh: 遺，失也。

translations.literal: Yi means lose.
translations.idiomatic: Yi (here) means to lose or let slip.

sentences.zh: 注[二]韋賢字長孺，魯國鄒人，治魯詩。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] Wei Xian, courtesy name Changru, was a man of Zou in the Lu state, studied the Lu Odes.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Wei Xian, courtesy name Changru, a native of Zou in Lu, was a master of the Lu tradition of the Classic of Poetry.

sentences.zh: 蔡義，河內溫人也，為韓詩，給事中也。

translations.literal: Cai Yi, a man of Wen in Henei, practiced Han Odes, was palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yi of Wen in Henei specialized in the Han Odes and served as a palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 夏侯勝，魯人也，字長公，治歐陽尚書。

translations.literal: Xiahou Sheng, a man of Lu, courtesy name Changgong, studied Ouyang Documents.
translations.idiomatic: Xiahou Sheng of Lu, courtesy name Changgong, was a master of the Ouyang Book of Documents.

sentences.zh: 並見前書。

translations.literal: All see the former book.
translations.idiomatic: All are treated in the earlier Han history.

sentences.zh: 注[三]酺等並自有傳。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] Pu and the rest each have their own biography.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Yu Pu and the others named here have separate biographies.

sentences.zh: 郁經授二帝，恩寵甚篤，賞賜前後數百千萬，顯於當世。

translations.literal: Yu by the classics instructed two emperors; grace and favor were very thick; rewards and grants before and after several hundred thousand and million, illustrious in the current generation.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Yu taught two emperors from the classics and basked in extraordinary favor; gifts in cash and silk ran into the millions, and he stood at the apex of his age.

sentences.zh: 門人楊震、朱寵，皆至三公。

translations.literal: Gate men Yang Zhen and Zhu Chong, both reached the three excellencies.
translations.idiomatic: Among his pupils Yang Zhen and Zhu Chong rose to the three highest offices.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]鄧騭傳曰：「朱寵字仲威，京兆人也。

translations.literal: [One] Commentary [one] Deng Zhi's biography states: "Zhu Chong, courtesy name Zhongwei, was a man of the capital region.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Note 1: Deng Zhi's biography identifies Zhu Chong, courtesy name Zhongwei, as a man of the metropolitan region.

sentences.zh: 篤行好學，從桓榮受尚書，位至太尉。」

translations.literal: He was earnest in conduct and loved learning, from Huan Rong received the Documents, rank reached grand commandant."
translations.idiomatic: Scrupulous in conduct and devoted to study, he learned the Documents from Huan Rong and rose to grand commandant."

sentences.zh: 初，榮受朱普學章句四十萬言，浮辭繁長，多過其實。

translations.literal: At first, Rong received Zhu Pu's learning chapter-and-sentence four hundred thousand words, floating phrases numerous and long, much exceeded the reality.
translations.idiomatic: At first Huan Rong had learned Zhu Pu's commentary on the Documents—four hundred thousand characters of inflated verbiage that often overshot the sense.

sentences.zh: [一]及榮入授顯宗，減為二十三萬言。

translations.literal: [One] When Rong entered to instruct the Manifest Ancestor, it was reduced to two hundred thirty thousand words.
translations.idiomatic: [1] When he taught Emperor Ming he pared it to two hundred thirty thousand characters.

sentences.zh: 郁復刪省定成十二萬言。

translations.literal: Yu again deleted and abbreviated, fixed and completed one hundred twenty thousand words.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Yu cut and condensed it again until a stable text of one hundred twenty thousand characters remained.

sentences.zh: 由是有桓君大小太常章句。

translations.literal: Thereby there were Lord Huan's large and small Grand Regular chapter-and-sentence.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the two recensions known as the elder and younger Lord Huan's minister-of-rituals commentary.

sentences.zh: 注[一]長音直亮反。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] chang is read zhi-liang fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The character chang ("long") is read with the fanqie zhi-liang.

sentences.zh: 子普嗣，傳爵至曾孫。

translations.literal: His son Pu succeeded, transmitted the noble rank to great-grandson.
translations.idiomatic: His son Huan Pu inherited the title, which passed down to a great-grandson.

sentences.zh: 郁中子焉，能世傳其家學。

translations.literal: Yu's middle son Yan, was able generationally to transmit his family's learning.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Yu's middle son, Huan Yan, carried the family learning into the next generation.

sentences.zh: [一]孫鸞、曾孫彬，並知名。

translations.literal: [One] Grandsons Luan and great-grandsons Bin, both were known by name.
translations.idiomatic: [1] His grandsons Huan Luan and great-grandsons such as Huan Bin also won renown.

sentences.zh: 注[一]華嶠書曰：「郁六子，普、延、焉、俊、酆、良。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] Hua Qiao's book states: "Yu had six sons: Pu, Yan, Yan, Jun, Feng, Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Hua Qiao names six sons: Pu, Yan, Yan, Jun, Feng, and Liang—that is, Huan Pu, Huan Yan, Huan Yan, and their younger brothers.

sentences.zh: 普嗣侯，傳國至曾孫，絕。

translations.literal: Pu succeeded the marquis, transmitted the state to great-grandson, cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Pu inherited the marquisate; the line ran to a great-grandson and then failed.

sentences.zh: 酆、良子孫皆博學有才能。」

translations.literal: Feng and Liang's sons and grandsons all were broadly learned and had ability and talent."
translations.idiomatic: The descendants of Feng and Liang remained learned men of ability."

sentences.zh: 焉字叔元，少以父任為郎。

translations.literal: Yan, courtesy name Shuyuan, in youth by father's appointment became gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Yan, courtesy name Shuyuan, entered the palace as a gentleman cadet through his father's rank.

sentences.zh: 明經篤行，有名稱。

translations.literal: Clear in the classics, earnest in conduct, he had reputation and title.
translations.idiomatic: He was celebrated for classical learning and steadfast conduct.

sentences.zh: 永初元年，入授安帝，三遷為侍中步兵校尉。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongchu, he entered to instruct Emperor An, thrice moved to palace attendant colonel of foot soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: In 107 CE he went to the palace to teach Emperor An, and after three promotions he was palace attendant and colonel of foot soldiers.

sentences.zh: 永寧中，順帝立為皇太子，以焉為太子少傅，月餘，遷太傅，以母憂自乞，聽以大夫行喪。

translations.literal: In Yongning, Emperor Shun was established as heir apparent; they made Yan heir junior tutor; after a month more, moved to grand tutor; by mother's mourning he begged his person; permitted to observe mourning as grandee.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yongning era, when Emperor Shun was named crown prince, Huan Yan became his junior tutor; a month later he was promoted to grand tutor. He asked leave to mourn his mother and was allowed to keep only his grandee's title while in mourning.

sentences.zh: 踰年，詔使者賜牛酒，奪服，即拜光祿大夫，遷太常。

translations.literal: Crossing the year, an edict ordered envoys to grant ox and wine, stripped mourning garments, immediately appointed supernumerary grandee, moved to grand regular.
translations.idiomatic: When the mourning year ended, envoys brought him oxen and wine, his mourning dress was formally removed, and he was at once named supernumerary grandee and then minister of rituals.

sentences.zh: 時廢皇太子為濟陰王，焉與太僕來歷、廷尉張□諫，不能得，事已具來歷傳。

translations.literal: At that time the deposed heir apparent became king of Jiyin; Yan with grand coachman Lai Li, chief judge Zhang Hao remonstrated, could not obtain; the affair is fully in Lai Li's biography.
translations.idiomatic: When the crown prince was demoted to prince of Jiyin, Huan Yan joined Grand Coachman Lai Li and Judge Zhang Hao in protesting; they failed to reverse the decision, as recounted in the biography of Lai Li.

sentences.zh: 視事三年，坐辟召禁錮者為吏免。

translations.literal: In office three years, by crime of employing as clerk one who was summoned while under prohibition and confinement, dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: After three years in office he was dismissed for appointing a man still under the ban on officeholding to a clerical post.

sentences.zh: 復拜光祿大夫。

translations.literal: Again appointed supernumerary grandee.
translations.idiomatic: He was soon reappointed supernumerary grandee.

sentences.zh: 陽嘉二年，代來歷為大鴻臚，數日，遷為太常。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yangjia, he replaced Lai Li as grand herald; after several days, moved to grand regular.
translations.idiomatic: In 133 CE he succeeded Lai Li as grand herald, then within days was transferred back to minister of rituals.

sentences.zh: 永和五年，代王龔為太尉。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yonghe, he replaced Wang Gong as grand commandant.
translations.idiomatic: In 140 CE he replaced Wang Gong as grand commandant.

sentences.zh: 漢安元年，以日食免。

translations.literal: In the first year of Han'an, by solar eclipse dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: In 142 CE he stepped down after a solar eclipse was laid to ministers' fault.

sentences.zh: 明年，卒於家。

translations.literal: The next year, he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home the following year.

sentences.zh: 注[一]省猶視也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: sheng (the graph usually read sheng in this gloss) is glossed as shi, meaning "to inspect" or "to oversee."
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The commentary glosses the character sheng with shi: to look after or oversee one's duties.

sentences.zh: 弟子傳業者數百人，黃瓊、楊賜最為顯貴。

translations.literal: Disciples who transmitted the occupation were several hundred persons; Huang Qiong and Yang Ci were most illustrious and noble.
translations.idiomatic: Several hundred students carried on his school; Huang Qiong and Yang Ci rose highest.

sentences.zh: 焉孫典。

translations.literal: Yan's grandson Dian.
translations.idiomatic: His grandson was Huan Dian.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]華嶠書曰：「焉長子衡，早卒。

translations.literal: [One] Commentary [one] Hua Qiao's book states: "Yan's eldest son Heng died early.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Note 1: Hua Qiao records that Huan Yan's eldest son Heng died young.

sentences.zh: 中子順，順子典。」

translations.literal: Middle son Shun; Shun's son Dian."
translations.idiomatic: The middle son was Shun; Shun's son was Dian."

sentences.zh: 典字公雅，復傳其家業，[一]以尚書教授穎川，門徒數百人。

translations.literal: Dian, courtesy name Gongya, again transmitted his family's occupation, [one] by the Documents instructed Yingchuan; gate disciples several hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Dian, courtesy name Gongya, continued the family tradition, teaching the Book of Documents in Yingchuan to several hundred pupils.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉為郎。

translations.literal: Recommended filial and incorrupt, became gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended as filial and incorrupt, he was appointed a gentleman.

sentences.zh: 居無幾，會國相王吉以罪被誅，[二]故人親戚莫敢至者。

translations.literal: Dwelling not long, it happened the state chancellor Wang Ji by crime was executed, [two] old friends and kin none dared arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward the chancellor of Pei, Wang Ji, was executed for a crime, and none of his former friends or relatives dared come near.

sentences.zh: 典獨□官收斂歸葬，服喪三年，負土成墳，為立祠堂，盡禮而去。

translations.literal: Dian alone resigned office, gathered and buried returning burial, observed mourning three years, carried earth completing the mound, for him established a shrine hall, exhausted ritual then departed.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Dian alone resigned his post, collected the body, escorted the coffin home, and mourned three years. He shouldered earth until the tomb stood high, built a shrine to Wang, observed every rite, and only then left.

sentences.zh: 注[一]華嶠書曰「典十二喪父母，事叔母如事親。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] Hua Qiao's book states "Dian at twelve mourned parents, served aunt-mother as serving parents.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Hua Qiao says Huan Dian lost his parents at twelve and thereafter honored his aunt as a mother.

sentences.zh: 立廉操，不取於人，門生故吏問遺，一無所受」也。

translations.literal: Established incorrupt conduct, took not from others; gate students old clerks asked and gifted, one all did not receive" also.
translations.idiomatic: He lived frugally and took nothing from others; when pupils or old subordinates brought gifts, he refused every one."

sentences.zh: 注[二]沛相。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] chancellor of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Wang Ji was chancellor of Pei.

sentences.zh: 辟司徒袁隗府，舉高第，拜侍御史。

translations.literal: Summoned to Excellency Yuan Sui's office, recommended of high rank, appointed attendant censor.
translations.idiomatic: He was summoned to the staff of Minister Yuan Sui, graduated at the top of the class, and named attendant censor.

sentences.zh: 是時宦官秉權，典執政無所迴避。

translations.literal: At that time eunuchs grasped power; Dian in holding government had nothing he circuited or avoided.
translations.idiomatic: Eunuchs held the reins of power, yet Huan Dian in office showed no fear of them.

sentences.zh: 常乘驄馬，京師畏憚，為之語曰：「行行且止，避驄馬御史。

translations.literal: He constantly rode a grey horse; the capital feared and dreaded; for him they made a saying: "Walk walk and stop—avoid the grey-horse censor."
translations.idiomatic: He always rode a grey horse, and the capital was so wary of him that people said, "Move along if you must, but halt if you see the censor on the grey horse."

sentences.zh: 」及黃巾賊起滎陽，典奉使督軍。

translations.literal: " When the Yellow Turban bandits rose at Xingyang, Dian by mission supervised troops.
translations.idiomatic: When the Yellow Turbans broke out at Xingyang, he was sent to oversee the troops.

sentences.zh: 賊破，還，以啎宦官賞不行。

translations.literal: When the bandits were broken, returning, by offending eunuchs reward did not proceed.
translations.idiomatic: After the rebels were crushed he returned, but because he had crossed the eunuchs his reward was never granted.

sentences.zh: 在御史七年不調，[一]後出為郎。

translations.literal: In the censorate seven years without transfer, [one] later went out as gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: He languished seven years in the censorate without promotion, [1] then was transferred out as a gentleman.

sentences.zh: 注[一]華嶠書作「十年」。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] Hua Qiao's book writes "ten years."
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Hua Qiao's text says ten years instead of seven.

sentences.zh: 靈帝崩，大將軍何進秉政，典與同謀議，三遷羽林中郎將。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ling died, great general He Jin grasped government; Dian with him plotted counsel, thrice moved to colonel of the Yulin guard.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Ling died, General He Jin seized power; Huan Dian joined his councils and rose thrice to colonel of the Yulin guard.

sentences.zh: [一]獻帝即位，三公奏典前與何進謀誅閹官，功雖不遂，忠義炳著。

translations.literal: [One] When Emperor Xian ascended, the three excellencies memorialized that Dian earlier with He Jin plotted to execute the palace officials; merit though not completed, loyalty and righteousness shone bright.
translations.idiomatic: [1] At Emperor Xian's accession the Three Excellencies reported that Huan Dian had earlier conspired with He Jin to purge the eunuchs; though the coup failed, his loyalty was beyond question.

sentences.zh: 詔拜家一人為郎，賜錢二十萬。

translations.literal: An edict appointed one person in his household gentleman, granted cash two hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: An edict named one member of his household a gentleman of the palace and gave him two hundred thousand cash.

sentences.zh: 注[一]華嶠書曰「遷平津都尉、鉤盾令、羽林中郎將」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] Hua Qiao's book states "moved to Pingjin commandant, hook-shield director, colonel of the Yulin guard" also.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Hua Qiao adds promotions to commandant of Pingjin, director of the hook-shield office, and colonel of the Yulin guard.

sentences.zh: 從西入關，拜御史中丞，賜爵關內侯。

translations.literal: Following west entering the passes, appointed palace secretary, granted noble rank marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: He followed the court west through the passes, was named imperial secretary, and enfeoffed as marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 車駕都許，遷光祿勳。

translations.literal: Imperial carriage capital at Xu, moved to superintendent of the household.
translations.idiomatic: When the capital moved to Xu he became superintendent of the imperial household.

sentences.zh: 建安六年，卒官。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Jian'an, died in office.
translations.idiomatic: He died in office in 201 CE.

sentences.zh: 鸞字始春，焉弟子也。

translations.literal: Luan, courtesy name Shichun, was Yan's disciple.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Luan, courtesy name Shichun, had studied under Huan Yan.

sentences.zh: [一]少立操行，褞袍糟食，不求盈餘。

translations.literal: [One] In youth he established conduct and practice, wore padded robe and coarse food, did not seek surplus.
translations.idiomatic: [1] From youth he lived plainly—patched cloak, coarse fare—and never chased wealth.

sentences.zh: [二]以世濁，州郡多非其人，恥不肯仕。

translations.literal: [Two] Because the age was turbid, provinces and commanderies mostly were not the right men; he was ashamed and would not serve.
translations.idiomatic: [2] The times were corrupt and local posts went to unworthy men; he thought it shameful to take office.

sentences.zh: 注[一]東觀記曰「鸞父良，龍舒侯相」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] the Eastern Lodge Record states "Luan's father Liang was minister of Longshu" also.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Eastern Lodge Record gives his father as Huan Liang, minister of Longshu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]東觀記曰「鸞貞亮之性，著乎幼沖。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] the Eastern Lodge Record states "Luan's chaste and bright nature showed in childhood.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The Eastern Lodge Record says his integrity showed even in boyhood.

sentences.zh: 學覽六經，莫不貫綜。

translations.literal: In learning he surveyed the six classics, none he did not thread together.
translations.idiomatic: He mastered the Six Classics from end to end.

sentences.zh: 推財孤寡，分賄友朋。

translations.literal: He pushed wealth to orphans and widows, divided bribes to friends.
translations.idiomatic: He gave his surplus to widows and orphans and shared gifts among friends.

sentences.zh: 泰於待賢，狹於養己。

translations.literal: Generous in treating the worthy, narrow in nurturing himself.
translations.idiomatic: Lavish toward worthy men, he stinted himself.

sentences.zh: 常著大布褞袍，糲食醋餐」也。

translations.literal: He constantly wore a great-cloth padded robe, coarse grain and vinegar meals" also.
translations.idiomatic: He went about in a heavy homespun cloak and lived on coarse grain and vinegar."

sentences.zh: 曄字文林，一名嚴，[一]尤修志介。

translations.literal: Ye, courtesy name Wenlin, one name Yan, [one] especially cultivated resolve and integrity.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Ye, courtesy name Wenlin, also called Yan, [1] was famous for fierce integrity.

sentences.zh: 姑為司空楊賜夫人。

translations.literal: His aunt was Minister Yang Ci's wife.
translations.idiomatic: His aunt was the wife of Minister Yang Ci.

sentences.zh: 初鸞卒，姑歸寧赴哀，將至，止於傳捨，整飾從者而後入，曄心非之。

translations.literal: At first when Luan died, the aunt returned to natal home to attend mourning; about to arrive, stopped at relay inn, arranged and adorned followers then afterward entered; Ye in heart faulted this.
translations.idiomatic: When Huan Luan died, his aunt set out from her husband's house to mourn; she halted at a post station to dress her retinue before entering, and Huan Ye inwardly condemned the display.

sentences.zh: 及姑勞問，終無所言，號哭而已。

translations.literal: When the aunt consoled and inquired, to the end he had no words, wailed and wept only.
translations.idiomatic: When she offered condolences he answered nothing—only wept.

sentences.zh: 賜遣吏奉祠，因縣發取祠具，曄拒不受。

translations.literal: Ci sent a clerk to perform sacrifice; thereby the county requisitioned sacrificial implements; Ye refused and did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Ci sent an officer to offer sacrifice, and the county tried to requisition the vessels from Huan Ye; he refused.

sentences.zh: 後每至京師，未嘗捨宿楊氏。

translations.literal: Later whenever he reached the capital, never lodged overnight at the Yang clan.
translations.idiomatic: After that he never spent a night under the Yang roof when he visited the capital.

sentences.zh: 其貞忮若此。

translations.literal: His chaste zhi was like this.
translations.idiomatic: Such was the rigor of his principles.

sentences.zh: [二]賓客從者，皆祗其志行，一餐不受於人。

translations.literal: [Two] Guests and followers all revered his intent and conduct; one meal he would not receive from others.
translations.idiomatic: [2] His retainers took his austerity to heart and would not accept even a single meal from outsiders.

sentences.zh: 仕為郡功曹。

translations.literal: He served as commandery merit assessor.
translations.idiomatic: He held the post of merit assessor in his commandery.

sentences.zh: 後舉孝廉、有道、方正、茂才，三公並辟，皆不應。

translations.literal: Later recommended filial and incorrupt, possessed of the Way, upright and true, flourishing talent; the three excellencies all summoned him; all did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: He was recommended filial and incorrupt, as a man of the Way, as upright talent, and as flourishing talent; the Three Excellencies summoned him repeatedly, and each time he declined.

sentences.zh: 注[一]東觀記「嚴」作「礹」。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] the Eastern Lodge Record writes "Yan" as "yan" (stone).
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Eastern Lodge text writes the alternate name with the "stone" radical.

sentences.zh: 注[二]忮，堅也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] zhi means hard.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Zhi here means unyielding or adamant.

sentences.zh: 初平中，天下亂，避地會稽，遂浮海客交址，[一]越人化其節，至閭裡不爭訟。

translations.literal: In Chuping, all under heaven was chaotic; he avoided the land in Kuaiji, then floated the sea as guest to Jiaozhi, [one] the Yue people transformed by his integrity, to hamlet lanes did not wrangle lawsuits.
