translations.idiomatic: During the Chuping era he fled the turmoil to Kuaiji, then crossed the sea to sojourn in Jiaozhi; [1] the local people were so moved by his example that quarrels ceased even in the lanes.

sentences.zh: 為凶人所誣，遂死於合浦獄。

translations.literal: By evil men he was slandered; thereupon died in Hepu prison.
translations.idiomatic: Slandered by villains, he died in the Hepu jail.

sentences.zh: 注[一]東觀記曰「礹到吳郡，揚州刺史劉繇振給谷食衣服所乏者，悉不受。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] the Eastern Lodge Record states "When Yan reached Wu commandery, Yang province inspector Liu Yao supplied grain and food and clothes of what he lacked; all he did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Eastern Lodge Record says that when Huan Ye reached Wu, Inspector Liu Yao of Yangzhou offered grain, clothing, and whatever he lacked, and he refused everything.

sentences.zh: 後東適會稽，住止山陰縣故魯相鐘離意捨，太守王朗餉給徹食﹑布帛﹑牛羊，一無所*

translations.literal: Later east went to Kuaiji, lodged stopped at Shanyin county former Lu minister Zhong Li Yi's house; prefect Wang Lang supplied clear food, cloth and silk, sheep and cattle; one nothing [text corrupt]
translations.idiomatic: He then traveled east to Kuaiji and lodged in Shanyin at the house once used by Zhong Li Yi, minister to the prince of Lu. Prefect Wang Lang sent cooked food, cloth, sheep, and cattle.

translations.literal: (ought)
translations.idiomatic: Editorial gloss in the manuscript: the graph should read dang, meaning "it ought to."

translations.literal: **[retain]*.
translations.idiomatic: The manuscript tradition is damaged here; he accepted none of it."

sentences.zh: 臨去之際，屋中尺寸之物，悉疏付主人，纖微不漏。

translations.literal: On the verge of departure, things in the house to a foot's measure, all listed and handed to the host; fine thread not leaking.
translations.idiomatic: When he left, he inventoried every stick of furniture for his host and omitted not the smallest item.

sentences.zh: 移居揚州從事屈豫室中，中庭橘樹一株，遇實孰，乃以竹藩樹四面，風吹落兩實，以繩繫著樹枝。

translations.literal: He moved to lodge in Yangzhou clerk Qu Yu's chamber; in the courtyard one orange tree, when fruit ripened, then by bamboo fence fenced the tree on four sides; wind blew down two fruits; he tied them with cord attaching to tree branches.
translations.idiomatic: He next stayed with clerk Qu Yu of Yangzhou. A single orange tree stood in the courtyard; when the fruit ripened he fenced it with bamboo. Wind shook down two oranges, and he tied them back to the branches with string.

sentences.zh: 每當危亡之急，其志彌固，賓客從者皆肅其行」也。

translations.literal: Whenever facing danger and ruin's urgent moment, his intent was the more firm; guests and followers all revered his conduct" also.
translations.idiomatic: In every crisis his resolution only hardened, and those who followed him respected his conduct."

sentences.zh: 彬字彥林，焉之兄孫也。

translations.literal: Bin, courtesy name Yanlin, was Yan's elder brother's grandson.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Bin, courtesy name Yanlin, was a grandson of Huan Yan's elder brother.

sentences.zh: 父麟，字符鳳，早有才惠。

translations.literal: Father Lin, courtesy name Zifeng, early had talent and cleverness.
translations.idiomatic: His father Huan Lin, courtesy name Zifeng, showed brilliance from an early age.

sentences.zh: [一]桓帝初，為議郎，入侍講禁中，以直道啎左右，出為許令，[二]病免。

translations.literal: [One] At the start of Emperor Huan's reign, was gentleman consultant, entered to attend exposition within the forbidden quarters, by straight way offended those at his side, went out as magistrate of Xu, [two] by illness dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Early in Emperor Huan's reign he was a gentleman consultant and lectured inside the palace; his bluntness offended favorites and he was sent out as magistrate of Xu, [2] then dismissed on grounds of illness.

sentences.zh: 會母終，麟不勝喪，未祥而卒，年四十一。

translations.literal: It happened his mother ended; Lin could not bear the mourning; before the second anniversary he died, age forty-one.
translations.idiomatic: When his mother died, Huan Lin's grief overwhelmed him; he died before the second anniversary of her death, aged forty-one.

sentences.zh: 所著碑﹑誄﹑贊﹑說﹑書凡二十一篇。

translations.literal: What he wrote: stele, dirge, praise, discourse, letters, in all twenty-one pieces.
translations.idiomatic: His writings—twenty-one pieces of epigraphy, dirges, encomia, essays, and letters—survived him.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]華嶠書曰「酆生麟」也。

translations.literal: [Three] Commentary [one] Hua Qiao's book states "Feng's son Lin" also.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Note 1: Hua Qiao identifies him as the son of Huan Feng.

sentences.zh: 注[二]許，縣名，今許州許昌縣也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] Xu is a county name; now Xuzhou Xuchang county.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Xu is the county that is now Xuchang in Xuzhou.

sentences.zh: 注[三]案摯虞文章志，麟文見在者十八篇，有碑九首，誄七首，七說一首，沛相郭府君書一首。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] checking Zhi Yu's Literary Records, Lin's writings extant are eighteen pieces: there are nine steles, seven dirges, one seven-discourse, one letter to minister of Pei lord Guo.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Zhi Yu's catalogue lists eighteen of Huan Lin's works still extant: nine stele inscriptions, seven dirges, one "Seven Discourses," and one letter to the minister of Pei, Lord Guo.

sentences.zh: 所著七說及書凡三篇，蔡邕等共論序其志，僉以為彬有過人者四：夙智早成，岐嶷也；

translations.literal: What he wrote seven-discourse and letters in all three pieces; Cai Yong and the rest together discussed and ordered his intent; all thought Bin had four surpassing others: early wisdom completed, qi-yi;
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong and others discussed the three surviving pieces—the "Seven Discourses" and letters—and agreed that Huan Bin surpassed other men in four ways. The first was precocious wisdom, the "keen and knowing" bearing praised in the Classic of Poetry.

sentences.zh: [一]學優文麗，至通也；

translations.literal: [One] learning excellent writing beautiful, utmost penetration;
translations.idiomatic: [1] The second was learning of depth and prose of grace—true mastery of the tradition.

sentences.zh: 仕不苟祿，絕高也；

translations.literal: serving not carelessly for salary, utmost height;
translations.idiomatic: The third was that he would not take office merely for the stipend—loftiness of aim.

sentences.zh: 辭隆從窊，絜操也。

translations.literal: declining eminence following the low, pure conduct.
translations.idiomatic: The fourth was that he turned down exalted rank to accept a lowly post—conduct of crystalline purity.

sentences.zh: [二] 乃共樹碑而頌焉。

translations.literal: [Two] Thereupon together they raised a stele and eulogized him.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Together they set up a stele in his honor.

sentences.zh: 注[一]夙，早也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] su means early.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Su means "from childhood."

sentences.zh: 岐，行魍也。

translations.literal: Qi: the received commentary text is corrupt; it alludes to the Odes phrase "ke qi ke yi" on a child's early bearing.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The line glossing qi is damaged in transmission; it echoes the Odes image of an infant already showing discernment.

sentences.zh: 嶷然有所識也。

translations.literal: Yi-ran: there is that which he recognizes.
translations.idiomatic: Yi marks the moment a child already shows discernment.

sentences.zh: 詩曰「克岐克嶷」也。

translations.literal: The Odes states "able qi able yi" also.
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry speaks of a child "keen and knowing" in the same sense.

sentences.zh: 注[二]窊，下也，音烏瓜反。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] wa means low; sound wu-gua fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Wa means "lowly"; it is read wu-gua in the fanqie spelling.

sentences.zh: 論曰：伏氏自東西京相襲為名儒，以取爵位。

translations.literal: The appraisal states: the Fu clan from Western and Eastern capitals in succession were famous Confucians, thereby taking noble rank.
translations.idiomatic: The judgment: the Fu family of Chang'an and Luoyang produced noted scholars generation after generation and rose to high rank.

sentences.zh: [一]中興而桓氏尤盛，自榮至典，世宗其道，父子兄弟代作帝師，受其業者皆至卿相，顯乎當世。

translations.literal: [One] At restoration the Huan clan was especially flourishing; from Rong to Dian, generations took their doctrine as ancestor; father and son and brothers generationally acted as imperial teachers; those who received their learning all reached ministers and chancellors, illustrious in the current generation.
translations.idiomatic: [1] After the restoration none matched the Huans: from Huan Rong to Huan Dian they handed down one school, and fathers, sons, and brothers served turn after turn as tutor to the throne; their pupils became ministers and stood at the center of public life.

sentences.zh: [孔]子曰：「古之學者為己，今之學者為人。

translations.literal: The Master stated: "The ancients' learners were for self; today's learners are for others."
translations.idiomatic: The Master said, "The men of old studied for their own sake; men today study for show."

sentences.zh: 」[二]為人者，憑譽以顯物；

translations.literal: " [Two] For others: relying on fame to display things;
translations.idiomatic: [2] To study for others is to trade on reputation;

sentences.zh: 為己者，因心以會道。

translations.literal: for self: by the heart to meet the Way.
translations.idiomatic: to study for oneself is to let the heart find the Way.

sentences.zh: 桓榮之累世見宗，豈其為己乎！

translations.literal: Huan Rong's accumulated generations being taken as model—could it be they were for self!
translations.idiomatic: Given the Master's distinction, was the long glory of the Huan line really a case of learning purely for one's own moral sake?

sentences.zh: 注[一]謂伏生已後至伏湛也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] means from Fu Sheng afterward down to Fu Zhan.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: That is, from Fu Sheng down to Fu Zhan in the Later Han.

sentences.zh: 注[二]論語文也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] Analects text.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The quotation is from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 丁鴻字孝公，穎川定陵人也。

translations.literal: Ding Hong, courtesy name Xiaogong, was a man of Dingling in Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Hong, courtesy name Xiaogong, came from Dingling in Yingchuan commandery.

sentences.zh: 父綝，字幼春，王莽末守穎陽尉。

translations.literal: Father Lin, courtesy name Youchun, at the end of Wang Mang guarded Yingyang assistant magistrate.
translations.idiomatic: His father Ding Lin, courtesy name Youchun, was assistant magistrate of Yingyang in Wang Mang's last years.

sentences.zh: 世祖略地穎陽，穎陽城守不下，綝說其宰，遂與俱降，世祖大喜，厚加賞勞，以綝為偏將軍，因從征伐。

translations.literal: The Founding Ancestor took territory at Yingyang; Yingyang city defended and would not submit; Lin persuaded its magistrate, thereupon with him together submitted; the Founding Ancestor greatly rejoiced, thickly added reward and labor, appointed Lin lieutenant general, thereby followed campaigns.
translations.idiomatic: When Guangwu was reducing Yingyang the city held out; Ding Lin talked its magistrate into surrendering with him. Delighted, Guangwu heaped rewards on him, named him lieutenant general, and took him on campaign.

sentences.zh: 綝將兵先度河，移檄郡國，攻營略地，下河南﹑陳留﹑穎川二十一縣。

translations.literal: Lin led troops first crossed the river, moved proclamations through commanderies and kingdoms, attacked camps and took territory, subdued Henan, Chenliu, Yingchuan twenty-one counties.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Lin led troops across the Yellow River first, sent summons through the commanderies, stormed camps and seized ground, and brought twenty-one counties of Henan, Chenliu, and Yingchuan to heel.

sentences.zh: 鴻年十三，從桓榮受歐陽尚書，三年而明章句，善論難，為都講，遂篤志精銳，布衣荷擔，不遠千里。

translations.literal: Hong at age thirteen, from Huan Rong received Ouyang Documents; in three years then understood chapter-and-sentence, was good at debating difficulties, became chief lecturer, thereupon fixed intent sharp and keen, in cloth clothes shouldering pole, not shying from a thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: At thirteen Ding Hong studied the Ouyang Book of Documents under Huan Rong; within three years he had mastered the glosses, excelled at disputation, and became chief lecturer. After that he devoted himself utterly, dressed as a commoner and staff on shoulder, and would travel a thousand li without hesitation.

sentences.zh: 初，綝從世祖征伐，鴻獨與弟盛居，憐盛幼小而共寒苦。

translations.literal: At first, Lin followed the Founding Ancestor on campaigns; Hong alone with younger brother Sheng dwelled, pitied Sheng's youth and smallness and together endured cold and hardship.
translations.idiomatic: While Ding Lin marched with Guangwu, Ding Hong stayed home with his younger brother Sheng, shielding the boy from cold and want.

sentences.zh: 及綝卒，鴻當襲封，上書讓國於盛，不報。

translations.literal: When Lin died, Hong ought to inherit the fief; he submitted a memorial yielding the state to Sheng; there was no reply.
translations.idiomatic: When Ding Lin died, Ding Hong was heir to the marquisate; he asked to pass the title to his younger brother Sheng, but the court never answered.

sentences.zh: 既葬，乃掛縗絰於頤廬而逃去，留書與盛曰：「鴻貪經書，不顧恩義，弱而隨師，[一]生不供養，死不飯唅，皇天先祖，並不佑助，身被大病，不任茅土。

translations.literal: After the burial, he hung his mourning bands on the tomb hut and fled, leaving a letter for Sheng saying: "Hong craves the classics and ignores grace and duty; in weakness I followed my teacher, [one] in life I did not support my parents, in death I did not place grain between their teeth—August Heaven and the ancestors together grant me no aid; I am stricken with grave illness and cannot bear the charge of fief."
translations.idiomatic: After the funeral he hung his mourning cords on the grave shed and ran away, leaving Ding Sheng a letter: "I have given my life to books and neglected family duty; I left home young to study, failed my parents in life, and could not close their mouths in death. Heaven and the ancestors withhold their blessing; I am gravely ill and unfit to hold a fief."

sentences.zh: [二]前上疾狀，願辭爵仲公，[三]章寢不報，迫且當襲封。

translations.literal: [Two] Earlier I submitted my illness and asked to yield the title to Zhonggong, [three] but my memorials went unanswered; I am pressed and must soon inherit the fief.
translations.idiomatic: [2] I have already memorialized my sickness and begged to cede the rank to Zhonggong, [3] yet no edict has come; the court still expects me to take the succession.

sentences.zh: 謹自放□，逐求良醫。

translations.literal: I respectfully cast myself aside and go in search of skilled physicians.
translations.idiomatic: I am laying down every office to seek a cure.

sentences.zh: 如遂不瘳，永歸溝壑。

translations.literal: If I am not healed, I shall end in ditch and gully.
translations.idiomatic: If I do not recover, I shall die on the road and trouble no one.

sentences.zh: 」鴻初與九江人鮑駿同事桓榮，甚相友善，及鴻亡封，與駿遇於東海，陽狂不識駿。

translations.literal: " At first Hong with Bao Jun of Jiujiang had both studied under Huan Rong and were close friends; when Hong fled his enfeoffment, he met Jun in Donghai and feigned madness, pretending not to know him.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Hong had studied with Bao Jun of Jiujiang under Huan Rong and they were fast friends; when Ding fled the succession, he met Bao in Donghai and played the madman, refusing to acknowledge him.

sentences.zh: 駿乃止而讓之曰：「昔伯夷﹑吳札亂世權行，故得申其志耳。

translations.literal: Jun then stopped him and rebuked him, saying: "In old days Boyi and Wu Zha acted expediently in a chaotic age—only thus could they carry out their intent.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Jun caught him and scolded him: "Boyi and Prince Jizha of Wu bent the rule in troubled times so they could follow their hearts."

sentences.zh: [四]春秋之義，不以家事廢王事。

translations.literal: [Four] The principle of the Spring and Autumn is: one does not set aside the king's business for family feeling.
translations.idiomatic: [4] The Spring and Autumn teaches that public duty outweighs private kinship."

sentences.zh: [五]今子以兄弟私恩而絕父不滅之基，可謂智乎？

translations.literal: [Five] Now you, for a brother's private affection, would cut off the foundation your father wished never to die—can that be called wisdom?"
translations.idiomatic: [5] You would sacrifice the line your father meant to preserve for brotherly sentiment—do you call that wisdom?"

sentences.zh: 」鴻感悟，垂涕歎息，乃還就國，開門教授。

translations.literal: " Hong was moved to insight, shed tears and sighed, then returned to take his state, opened his gate, and taught.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Hong broke down, wept, and went home to his fief, where he opened his doors to students.

sentences.zh: 鮑駿亦上書言鴻經學至行，顯宗甚賢之。

translations.literal: Bao Jun also submitted a memorial saying Hong's classical learning and supreme conduct were outstanding; the Manifest Ancestor greatly esteemed him.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Jun too memorialized on Ding Hong's scholarship and character, and Emperor Ming thought the world of him.

sentences.zh: [六] 注[一]弱，少也。

translations.literal: [Six] Commentary [one] ruo means young.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Ruo here means "in youth."

sentences.zh: 注[二]任，堪也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] ren means endure or bear.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Ren means "to be equal to" or "to bear."

sentences.zh: 注[三]仲公，盛之字也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] Zhonggong is Sheng's courtesy name.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Zhonggong is the courtesy name of Ding Sheng.

sentences.zh: 注[四]伯夷，孤竹君之子，讓其弟叔齊，餓死於首陽之山。

translations.literal: Commentary [four] Boyi was a son of the lord of Guzhu; he yielded to his younger brother Shuqi and starved on Mount Shouyang.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: Boyi was a prince of Guzhu who yielded to his brother Shuqi and died of hunger on Mount Shouyang.

sentences.zh: 吳札，吳王壽夢之季子也，諸兄欲讓其國，季子乃捨其室而耕。

translations.literal: Wu Zha was the youngest son of Wu king Shou Meng; his elder brothers wished to yield the state to him, so the youngest son abandoned his house and plowed.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Jizha of Wu was the youngest son of King Shou Meng; when his brothers tried to pass him the throne, he left his palace and took up the plough.

sentences.zh: 皆是權時所行，非常之道也。

translations.literal: All were expedient acts for the moment, not the constant Way.
translations.idiomatic: Those were exceptional measures for exceptional times, not a precedent for ordinary conduct.

sentences.zh: 伯夷當紂時，吳札當周之末，故言亂*

translations.literal: Boyi lived under the last kings of Shang; Wu Zha at the end of Zhou—hence the text says "in chaotic ages" (manuscript damaged).
translations.idiomatic: Boyi faced the fall of Shang and Jizha the decay of Zhou—hence the gloss speaks of "troubled ages"; the manuscript is damaged at this point.

translations.literal: (ye)
translations.idiomatic: The particle ye closes the gloss; the preceding graph is uncertain in transmission.
The commentary originally read "in chaotic ages."

sentences.zh: 注[五]春秋衛靈公卒，孫輒立，父蒯聵與輒爭國。

translations.literal: Commentary [five] When Duke Ling of Wei died, his grandson Ji was enthroned while his father Kuai Kui struggled with Ji for the state.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5: Duke Ling of Wei died, his grandson Ji came to the throne, and his father Kuai Kui fought him for power.

sentences.zh: 公羊傳曰：「輒者曷為？

translations.literal: The Guliang commentary asks: "What sort of man is Ji?"
translations.idiomatic: The Guliang asks, "Who is Ji?"

sentences.zh: 蒯聵之子。

translations.literal: Kuai Kui's son.
translations.idiomatic: He was Kuai Kui's son.

sentences.zh: 然則曷為不立蒯聵而立輒？

translations.literal: Why then was Kuai Kui not established, and Ji established instead?
translations.idiomatic: Why, then, was the father passed over in favor of the son?"

sentences.zh: 蒯聵無道，靈公逐之而立輒。

translations.literal: Kuai Kui lacked the Way; Duke Ling drove him out and enthroned Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Because Kuai Kui was unworthy, Duke Ling expelled him and set Ji on the throne."

sentences.zh: 然則輒之義可以立乎？

translations.literal: May Ji's claim to the throne be justified, then?
translations.idiomatic: Was Ji therefore right to reign?"

sentences.zh: 曰可。

translations.literal: The answer: Yes.
translations.idiomatic: The text answers: Yes."

sentences.zh: 不以父命辭於王命，不以家事辭於王事。

translations.literal: One does not reject the king's command on a father's order; one does not reject the king's business on account of family.
translations.idiomatic: A subject must not refuse the ruler's mandate for a father's word, nor set private family concerns above public duty."

sentences.zh: 」故駿引以為言也。

translations.literal: " Therefore Jun cited these words.
translations.idiomatic: That is the passage Bao Jun quoted to Ding Hong."

sentences.zh: 注[六]續漢書載駿書曰：「臣聞武王克殷，封比干之墓，表商容之閭，二人無功，下車先封之，表善顯仁，為國之砥礪也。

translations.literal: Commentary [six] the Continued Book of Han preserves Jun's memorial: "Your servant has heard that when King Wu conquered Yin, he restored Bi Gan's tomb and marked Shang Rong's lane—two men who had done him no service, yet he dismounted and honored them first, displaying goodness and benevolence as the whetstone of the state.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6: The Continued Han quotes Bao Jun: "When King Wu overthrew Yin, he repaired Bi Gan's grave and marked the lane of Shang Rong—men who had never aided him—because a ruler must hold up goodness as the state's grindstone.

sentences.zh: 伏見丁鴻經明行修，志節清妙。

translations.literal: I prostrate myself to report that Ding Hong's learning is lucid and his conduct cultivated, his purpose pure and refined."
translations.idiomatic: I submit that Ding Hong's scholarship is clear and his life stainless, his aims high and clean."

sentences.zh: 」由是上賢之也。

translations.literal: " Thereby the sovereign deemed him worthy.
translations.idiomatic: On that account the emperor honored Ding Hong.

sentences.zh: 永平十年詔征，鴻至即召見，說文侯之命篇，[一]賜御衣及綬，稟食公車，[二] 與博士同禮。

translations.literal: In the tenth year of Yongping an edict summoned him; Hong arrived and was received at once in audience, expounded the "Charge to the Marquis of Wen" chapter, [one] was granted imperial robes and ribbons, supplied with food at the public carriage office, [two] and treated with the same ceremony as the court scholars.
translations.idiomatic: In 67 CE an edict summoned him; as soon as he reached the capital he was admitted to audience, lectured on the "Charge to Wen" in the Documents, received court dress and seals, a stipend from the imperial messengers' hostel, [2] and rank equal to the chair-holders.

sentences.zh: 頃之，拜侍中。

translations.literal: Before long he was appointed palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Soon he became a palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 十三年，兼射聲校尉。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year he additionally held the colonelcy of the Archers' Voice.
translations.idiomatic: In 70 CE he was also named colonel of the Archers' Voice regiment.

sentences.zh: 建初四年，徙封魯陽鄉侯。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Jianchu his fief was moved to village marquis of Luyang.
translations.idiomatic: In 79 CE his enfeoffment was transferred to the village of Luyang.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]周平王東遷洛邑，晉文侯仇有輔佐之功，平王賜以車馬﹑弓矢而策命之，因以名篇，事見尚書也。

translations.literal: [Three] Commentary [one] when King Ping of Zhou moved east to Luoyi, Marquis Wen of Jin, You, had assisting merit; King Ping granted him chariots, horses, bow and arrows, and issued a written charge—thereby the piece takes its name; the affair is in the Documents.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Note 1: King Ping's eastern move to Luoyang and his charge to Marquis Wen of Jin, with its gifts of chariot, bow, and arrows, form the "Charge to Wen" chapter of the Book of Documents.

sentences.zh: 注[二]稟，給也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] bin means supply.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Bin means "to supply" or "to ration."

sentences.zh: 公車，署名，公車所在，因以名。

translations.literal: Public carriage is an office name; because the public carriage is located there, it is so named.
translations.idiomatic: The "public carriage" office took its name from the post where candidates for summons waited.

sentences.zh: 諸待詔者，皆居以待命，故令給食焉。

translations.literal: All who awaited edict lodged there awaiting command; therefore he was ordered supplied with food.
translations.idiomatic: Men awaiting imperial appointment lived there, hence the court fed them.

sentences.zh: 注[三]東觀記曰：「魯陽鄉在尋陽*

translations.literal: The Eastern Lodge Record states: "The village of Luyang lies in Xunyang
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Lodge Record places the Luyang fief in Xunyang

translations.literal: (commandery)
translations.idiomatic: The gloss supplies the word commandery; the manuscript is defective here.

translations.literal: The fragment ends with the word county; the manuscript is damaged.
translations.idiomatic: The line should read that Luyang township lies in Xunyang commandery, in such-and-such a county, but the county name is lost in a lacuna in the text.

sentences.zh: 肅宗詔鴻與廣平王羨及諸儒樓望﹑成封﹑桓郁﹑賈逵等，論定五經同異於北宮白虎觀，[一]使五官中郎將魏應主承製問難，侍中淳於恭奏上，帝親稱制臨決。

translations.literal: The Solemn Ancestor edicted Hong together with Prince Xian of Guangping and the scholars Lou Wang, Cheng Feng, Huan Yu, Jia Kui, and others to settle the agreements and disagreements of the Five Classics at the White Tiger Hall of the Northern Palace, [one] ordering captain of the household guard Wei Ying to receive the emperor's questions and conduct the debate, palace attendant Chunyu Gong to memorialize the results, while the emperor himself pronounced judgment.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhang ordered Ding Hong, Prince Xian of Guangping, Lou Wang, Cheng Feng, Huan Yu, Jia Kui, and others to thrash out the Five Classics at the White Tiger Hall; Captain Wei Ying put the emperor's questions, Chunyu Gong reported the answers, and the sovereign gave the final ruling in his own voice.

sentences.zh: 鴻以才高，論難最明，諸儒稱之，帝數嗟美焉。

translations.literal: Hong by highest talent made the debate clearest; the scholars praised him, and the emperor repeatedly sighed in admiration.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Hong's brilliance carried the disputation; the Ru applauded him and the emperor often marveled aloud.

sentences.zh: 時人歎曰：「殿中無雙丁孝公。」

translations.literal: Men of the time exclaimed: "In the palace hall none can match Ding Xiaogong."
translations.idiomatic: Contemporaries said, "Under the palace roof no one rivals Ding Xiaogong."

sentences.zh: [二]數受賞賜，擢徙校書，遂代成封為少府。

translations.literal: [Two] He repeatedly received rewards, was promoted to collator of texts, and then succeeded Cheng Feng as junior steward.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Honors and gifts followed in succession; he rose to collator of the palace library and then replaced Cheng Feng as junior steward.

sentences.zh: 門下由是益盛，遠方至者數千人。

translations.literal: Thereby his school grew still greater; those who came from afar numbered in the thousands.
translations.idiomatic: His following swelled until several thousand students came from distant commanderies.

sentences.zh: 彭城劉愷﹑北海巴茂﹑九江朱倀皆至公卿。

translations.literal: Liu Kai of Pengcheng, Ba Mao of Beihai, and Zhu Zhang of Jiujiang all rose to the three highest offices.
translations.idiomatic: Among his pupils Liu Kai of Pengcheng, Ba Mao of Beihai, and Zhu Zhang of Jiujiang reached ministerial rank.

sentences.zh: 元和三年，徙封馬亭鄉侯。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yuanhe his fief was moved to village marquis of Mating.
translations.idiomatic: In 86 CE his title was transferred to the village of Mating.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]廣平王羨，明帝子也。

translations.literal: [Three] Commentary [one] Prince Xian of Guangping was a son of Emperor Ming.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Note 1: Prince Xian of Guangping was a son of Emperor Ming.

sentences.zh: 東觀記曰「與太常樓望﹑少府成封﹑屯騎校尉桓郁﹑衛士令賈逵等集議」也。

translations.literal: The Eastern Lodge Record states "with Grand Regular Lou Wang, Junior Steward Cheng Feng, Colonel of Garrison Cavalry Huan Yu, Guard commandant Jia Kui and others gathered to deliberate" also.
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Lodge names Grand Minister Lou Wang, Junior Steward Cheng Feng, Colonel Huan Yu, and Guard Director Jia Kui among those who sat in conference.

sentences.zh: 白虎，門名。

translations.literal: White Tiger is a gate name.
translations.idiomatic: "White Tiger" was the name of a palace gate.

sentences.zh: 於門立觀，因之以名焉。

translations.literal: At the gate they erected a hall; by it they named the place.
translations.idiomatic: A lecture hall was built beside that gate, which gave the conference its name.

sentences.zh: 注[二]東觀記曰：「上歎嗟其才，號之曰『殿中無雙丁孝公』，賜錢二十萬。」

translations.literal: Commentary [two] the Eastern Lodge Record states: "The sovereign sighed at his talent, styled him 'in the hall none match Ding Xiaogong,' and granted two hundred thousand cash."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The Eastern Lodge says the emperor himself bestowed the nickname and two hundred thousand cash.

sentences.zh: 續漢書亦同。

translations.literal: The Continued Book of Han is the same.
translations.idiomatic: The Continued Han agrees.

sentences.zh: 而此書獨作「時人歎」也。

translations.literal: But this book alone writes "men of the time sighed."
translations.idiomatic: Our text alone attributes the praise to "people of the time."

sentences.zh: 注[三]東觀記曰：「元和二年，車駕東巡狩，鴻以少府從。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] the Eastern Lodge Record states: "In the second year of Yuanhe the imperial carriage toured east on hunt; Hong as junior steward followed.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: In 85 CE, says the Eastern Lodge, the emperor toured the east and Ding Hong accompanied him as junior steward.

sentences.zh: 上奏曰：『臣聞古之帝王，統治天下，五載巡狩，至於岱宗，柴祭於天，望秩山川，協時月正日，同斗斛權衡，使人不爭。

translations.literal: He submitted: "Your servant has heard that the ancient kings who ruled all under heaven every five years toured on inspection, reaching Dai Mount, performed the firewood sacrifice to Heaven, ranked the mountains and rivers in worship, harmonized seasons, months, and the first day, unified bushels and balances, so that men did not wrangle."
translations.idiomatic: Ding Hong memorialized: "The sage-kings of old, ruling the realm, inspected the empire every five years, climbed Mount Tai to burn offerings to Heaven, sacrificed to the peaks and streams in due order, aligned the calendar, and standardized measures so that the people would not quarrel."

sentences.zh: 陛下尊履蒸蒸，奉承弘業，祀五帝於明堂，配以光武，二祖四宗，鹹有告祀。

translations.literal: Your Majesty in awesome filial piety inherits the great enterprise, sacrifices to the Five Thearchs in the Bright Hall with Guangwu as correlate—the two founders and four exalted ancestors all receive announcement rites.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty, steeped in filial devotion, carries on the grand work: you sacrifice to the Five Lords in the Bright Hall with Emperor Guangwu as correlative, and the two founders and four temple ancestors all receive their due offerings."

sentences.zh: 瞻望太山，嘉澤降澍，柴祭之日，白氣上升，與燎煙合，黃鵠腢翔，所謂神人以和，荅響之休符也。

translations.literal: Gazing on Mount Tai, fine rain fell in timely showers; on the day of the firewood sacrifice white vapor rose and merged with the smoke of the pyre, yellow swans wheeled in pairs—the so-called harmony of gods and men, the auspicious sign of answering resonance."
translations.idiomatic: When you turned toward Mount Tai, blessed rain fell; on the day of the burnt offering white mist rose to mingle with the smoke, and paired yellow cranes circled—the very token of accord between Heaven and man."

sentences.zh: 』上善焉。

translations.literal: " The sovereign approved it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor praised the memorial.

sentences.zh: 」又曰「以廬江郡為六安國」，所以徙封為馬亭侯。

translations.literal: " It also states "Lujiang commandery was made the Liu'an principality"—therefore the fief was moved to Mating marquis.
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Lodge Record adds that Lujiang commandery was reorganized as the principality of Liu'an, which prompted Ding Hong's transfer to the Mating marquisate."

sentences.zh: 和帝即位，遷太常。

translations.literal: When Emperor He ascended, Hong moved to grand regular.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor He came to the throne, Ding Hong became minister of rituals.

sentences.zh: 永元四年，代袁安為司徒。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Yongyuan he replaced Yuan An as minister of education.
translations.idiomatic: In 92 CE he succeeded Yuan An as minister of education.

sentences.zh: 是時竇太后臨政，憲兄弟各□威權。

translations.literal: At that time Empress Dowager Dou attended to government; the Dou brothers each grasped might and power.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Dou held court, and her brothers monopolized military and civil power.

sentences.zh: 鴻因日食，上封事曰：

translations.literal: Hong by reason of a solar eclipse submitted a sealed memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: After a solar eclipse Ding Hong submitted a sealed memorial:

sentences.zh: 臣聞日者陽精，守實不虧，君之象也；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that the sun is the essence of yang, guarding fullness without loss—the image of the ruler;
translations.idiomatic: The sun is the quintessence of yang: it keeps its fullness and is the image of the sovereign;

sentences.zh: 月者陰精，盈毀有常，臣之表也。

translations.literal: the moon is the essence of yin, waxing and waning by rule—the outward sign of ministers.
translations.idiomatic: the moon is the essence of yin, waxing and waning by rule, and mirrors the ministers.

sentences.zh: 故日食者，臣乘君，陰陵陽；

translations.literal: Therefore a solar eclipse means ministers riding the ruler, yin overwhelming yang;
translations.idiomatic: A solar eclipse therefore means subjects encroaching on the ruler, yin riding over yang;

sentences.zh: 月滿不虧，下驕盈也。

translations.literal: a full moon that does not wane means those below are arrogant and excessive.
translations.idiomatic: a full moon that fails to wane means pride and excess below.

sentences.zh: 昔周室衰季，皇甫之屬專權於外，黨類強盛，侵奪主埶，則日月薄食，[一]故詩曰：「十月之交，朔月辛卯，日有食之，亦孔之丑。

translations.literal: Formerly when the Zhou house declined in its last generation, the Huangfu group monopolized power outside; their faction grew strong, seized and plundered the ruler's position—then sun and moon drew near eclipse, [one] hence the Odes says: "In the tenth month's conjunction, on the first day, xin-mao, there was a solar eclipse—how ugly a sight."
translations.idiomatic: In the late Zhou, when favorites such as the Huangfu clan seized power abroad, sun and moon went dark together; [1] the Classic of Poetry cries, "On the new moon of the tenth month, the day xin-mao, came an eclipse—how dreadful an omen!"

sentences.zh: 」[二]春秋日食三十六，弒君三十二。

translations.literal: " [Two] The Spring and Autumn records thirty-six solar eclipses and thirty-two ruler-murders.
translations.idiomatic: [2] The Spring and Autumn records thirty-six eclipses and thirty-two regicides.

sentences.zh: 變不空生，各以類應。

translations.literal: Omens do not arise empty; each responds to its kind.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven does not send warnings for nothing; each portent answers to its moral class.

sentences.zh: 夫威柄不以放下，利器不可假人。

translations.literal: The handle of awe must not be handed downward; sharp tools must not be lent to others.
translations.idiomatic: The rod of authority must not be dropped into inferior hands, nor the state's sharp weapons lent out.

sentences.zh: [三]覽觀往古，近察漢興，傾危之禍，靡不由之。

translations.literal: [Three] Scanning antiquity and inspecting the rise of Han, every overturning disaster has come from this.
translations.idiomatic: [3] A glance at antiquity and at our own dynasty shows every catastrophe sprang from the same root.

sentences.zh: 是以三桓專魯，田氏□齊，六卿分晉；

translations.literal: Therefore the Three Huan monopolized Lu, the Tian clan swallowed Qi, the six ministers divided Jin;
translations.idiomatic: The Three Huan held Lu captive, the Tian house usurped Qi, the six ministerial clans carved up Jin;

sentences.zh: 諸呂握權，統嗣幾移；

translations.literal: the various Lü grasped power and the imperial succession nearly shifted;
translations.idiomatic: the Lü kin seized the government and the imperial line nearly changed hands;

sentences.zh: 哀﹑平之末，廟不血食。

translations.literal: at the end of Emperors Ai and Ping, the temples went without blood sacrifice.
translations.idiomatic: by the end of Emperors Ai and Ping the ancestral shrines stood cold.

sentences.zh: [四]故雖有周公之親，而無其德，不得行其埶也。

translations.literal: [Four] Therefore though one have the Duke of Zhou's closeness of kin, without his virtue one cannot wield his power.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Kinship as close as the Duke of Zhou's avails nothing without the Duke of Zhou's virtue.

sentences.zh: [五] 注[一]周室衰謂幽王時也。

translations.literal: [Five] Commentary [one] Zhou house's decline means the time of King You.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Note 1: "Decline of Zhou" refers to the reign of King You.

sentences.zh: 皇甫即幽王后之黨也。

translations.literal: Huangfu is the faction of King You's empress.
translations.idiomatic: The Huangfu ministers were the faction of Queen Bao of You.

sentences.zh: 詩小雅曰：「皇甫卿士，番惟司徒，家伯維宰，仲允膳夫。

translations.literal: The Odes minor ya says: "Huangfu was minister of works, Fan was steward of the ancestral temple, Jia Bo was steward of the interior, Zhong Yun was master of meats."
translations.idiomatic: The Minor Odes names them: "Huangfu was minister of works, Fan steward of the temple, Jia Bo steward of the interior, Zhong Yun master of meats."

sentences.zh: 」其類非一，故言之屬也。

translations.literal: " Their kind was not one; therefore it says "the group."
translations.idiomatic: They were not a single clan but a whole clique of favorites."

sentences.zh: 注[二]十月之交，詩小雅篇名也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] "Tenth month" is the title of a minor ya ode.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: "Tenth month" is the name of an ode in the Minor Odes.

sentences.zh: 孔，甚也。

translations.literal: Kong means very.
translations.idiomatic: Kong here means "greatly."

sentences.zh: 丑，惡也。

translations.literal: Chou means ugly or evil.
translations.idiomatic: Chou means "hideous" or "ill-omened."

sentences.zh: 周之十月，夏之八月也。

translations.literal: Zhou's tenth month is Xia's eighth month.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou tenth month corresponds to the eighth month of the Xia calendar.

sentences.zh: 八月朔，日月交而日食，陰侵陽，臣侵君之象也。

translations.literal: On the eighth month's new moon sun and moon crossed and there was a solar eclipse—yin encroaching on yang, ministers encroaching on the ruler's image.
translations.idiomatic: At the new moon of the eighth month the luminaries met and the sun was eaten—yin assailing yang, ministers overshadowing their lord.

sentences.zh: 日辰之義，日為君，辰為臣。

translations.literal: In the meaning of day and stem-branch, the day is the ruler, the stem-branch the minister.
translations.idiomatic: In calendrical symbolism the day stands for the ruler and the cyclical sign for his ministers.

sentences.zh: 辛，金也。

translations.literal: Xin is metal.
translations.idiomatic: The stem xin belongs to the element metal.

sentences.zh: 卯，木也。

translations.literal: Mao is wood.
translations.idiomatic: The branch mao belongs to wood.

sentences.zh: 又以卯侵金，故甚惡也。

translations.literal: Moreover mao encroaches on metal—hence it is very evil.
translations.idiomatic: Wood then overcomes metal, which makes the omen especially grim.

sentences.zh: 注[三]劉向上書云：「弒君三十六。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] Liu Xiang's memorial states: "Thirty-six ruler-murders."
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Liu Xiang's memorial counts thirty-six regicides.

sentences.zh: 」今據春秋與劉向同，而東觀及續漢范氏諸本皆云「三十二」，蓋誤也。

translations.literal: " Now according to the Spring and Autumn it agrees with Liu Xiang, but the Eastern Lodge and Continued Han Fan editions all say "thirty-two"—probably error.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn actually supports Liu Xiang's thirty-six; the figures of thirty-two in the Eastern Lodge and Fan Ye's text are mistaken.

sentences.zh: 威柄謂周禮之八柄，即爵﹑祿﹑生﹑置、予﹑奪﹑廢﹑誅也。

translations.literal: The handle of awe means the Zhou li's eight handles—namely rank, salary, life, appointment, grant, confiscation, dismissal, and execution.
translations.idiomatic: "The handle of awe" is the eight powers in the Rites of Zhou: conferring rank, salary, life, placement, gifts, confiscation, dismissal, and execution.

sentences.zh: 利器謂國之權埶。

translations.literal: Sharp tools means the state's power and position.
translations.idiomatic: "Sharp tools" means the coercive power of the state.

sentences.zh: 假，借也。

translations.literal: Jia means lend.
translations.idiomatic: Jia means "to lend."

sentences.zh: 左傳曰「唯器與名，不可以假人」也。

translations.literal: The Zuo commentary states "only vessels and titles may not be lent to others" also.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo zhuan warns that regalia and titles must never be lent away."

sentences.zh: 注[四]三桓謂季孫氏﹑叔孫氏﹑仲孫氏。

translations.literal: Commentary [four] the Three Huan mean the Ji-sun, Shu-sun, and Zhong-sun houses.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: The Three Huan are the Ji, Shu, and Meng clans of Lu.

sentences.zh: 三家皆出自魯桓公，故言三桓。

translations.literal: The three houses all issued from Duke Huan of Lu; therefore they are called the Three Huan.
translations.idiomatic: The Ji, Shu, and Meng houses were all cadet lines of Duke Huan of Lu, which is why historians speak of the Three Huan.

sentences.zh: 並專權魯國。

translations.literal: Together they monopolized power in the state of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Together they dominated the government of Lu.

sentences.zh: 至魯昭公，遂為季氏所逐，平子乃攝行君事。

translations.literal: Down to Duke Zhao of Lu, then he was driven out by the Ji clan; Pingzi then held the ruler's affairs in proxy.
translations.idiomatic: Under Duke Zhao the Ji drove the duke into exile, and Ji Pingzi ruled Lu in his name.

sentences.zh: 田氏，陳敬仲之後，因自陳奔齊，改為田氏，遂執齊政，至田和乃篡齊。

translations.literal: The Tian house, descendants of Chen Jingzhong, fled from Chen to Qi, changed to the surname Tian, then held Qi's government; down to Tian He then usurped Qi.
translations.idiomatic: The Tian lineage sprang from Chen Wan, who fled Qi for Chen and adopted the name Tian; his descendants took Qi from within until Tian He formally seized the throne.

sentences.zh: 六卿謂晉之智氏﹑中行氏﹑范氏﹑韓氏﹑趙氏﹑魏氏，並專晉政，韓﹑趙﹑魏卒三分晉國也。

translations.literal: The six ministers mean Jin's Zhi, Zhonghang, Fan, Han, Zhao, and Wei houses; together they monopolized Jin government; Han, Zhao, and Wei finally three-way divided the state of Jin.
translations.idiomatic: The six ministerial clans of Jin—the Zhi, Zhonghang, Fan, Han, Zhao, and Wei—partitioned ducal power until Han, Zhao, and Wei carved the old state into three successor realms.

sentences.zh: 諸呂謂呂產﹑呂祿也。

translations.literal: The various Lü mean Lü Chan and Lü Lu.
translations.idiomatic: "The various Lü" refers to Lü Chan and Lü Lu, the empress dowager's kin.

sentences.zh: 產領南軍，祿領北軍，謀危劉氏，故曰「統嗣幾移」。

translations.literal: Chan commanded the southern army, Lu the northern army; they plotted to endanger the Liu house—therefore it says "the imperial succession nearly shifted."
translations.idiomatic: Lü Chan led the southern garrison and Lü Lu the northern; their plot to topple the Liu dynasty is what the text means by the succession almost changing hands.

sentences.zh: 注[五]言親賢兼重，方可執政。

translations.literal: Commentary [five] the words mean kinship and worth must both be weighty before one may wield government.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5: The gloss recalls Mencius: only when both blood ties and moral weight suffice may a man govern.

sentences.zh: 孟子曰：「有伊尹之心則可，無伊尹之心則篡也。」

translations.literal: Mencius says: "If one has the heart of Yi Yin it is acceptable; without Yi Yin's heart it is usurpation."
translations.idiomatic: Mencius warns that power may be taken only with Yi Yin's disinterested intent; otherwise it is plain usurpation.

sentences.zh: 今大將軍雖欲□身自約，不敢僭差，然而天下遠近皆惶怖承旨，刺史二千石初除謁辭，求通待報，雖奉符璽，受台□，不敢便去，久者至數十日。

translations.literal: Now though the great general wishes to discipline himself and dare not overstep, yet all under heaven, far and near, tremble and carry out his orders: when governors and two-thousand-bushel officials first receive appointment they pay respects and beg leave, seek audience and await his word—even holding tallies and receiving terrace edicts they dare not depart at once; long delays reach several tens of days.
translations.idiomatic: Even if General Dou Xian claims to discipline himself and never overstep, every official from the capital to the marches waits in terror on his nod. Newly appointed governors must call on him before taking up duty; though they hold imperial tallies and secretariat edicts, they dare not leave his gate for weeks.

sentences.zh: 背王室，向私門，此乃上威損，下權盛也。

translations.literal: They turn their backs on the royal house and toward private gates—this is the superior's majesty diminished and the inferior's power swollen.
translations.idiomatic: Men face away from the throne and toward the Dou clan—proof that the sovereign's awe is waning while a subject's power swells.

sentences.zh: 人道悖於下，效驗見於天，雖有隱謀，神照其情，垂象見戒，以告人君。

translations.literal: When the human way is perverse below, the verification appears in Heaven; though there be hidden plots, spirit illuminates the motive; hanging portents show warnings to inform the lord.
translations.idiomatic: Moral disorder below shows in the sky above; secret schemes cannot hide from spiritual insight; omens are Heaven's memoranda to the throne.

sentences.zh: 閒者月滿先節，過望不虧，[一]此臣驕溢背君，專功獨行也。

translations.literal: Recently the moon was full before its season, passed the full moon without waning—[one] this is ministers arrogant and excessive, turning their backs on the ruler, monopolizing merit and acting alone.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Lately the moon has waxed full before its due and stayed round past the night of opposition—signs that ministers grow insolent, defy their lord, and arrogate every success.

sentences.zh: 陛下未深覺悟，故天重見戒，誠宜畏懼，以防其禍。

translations.literal: Your Majesty has not yet deeply awakened; therefore Heaven again shows warning—you should truly fear and guard against the calamity.
translations.idiomatic: Because Your Majesty has not taken the lesson to heart, Heaven repeats its warning; you must meet it with dread and cut off the disaster at the root.

sentences.zh: 詩云：「敬天之怒，不敢戲豫。

translations.literal: The Odes says: "Revere Heaven's anger; dare not be playful or at ease."
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry says, "Stand in awe of Heaven's wrath and never treat it lightly."

sentences.zh: 」[二]若□政責躬，杜漸防萌，則凶妖銷滅，害除福湊矣。

translations.literal: "[Two] If you cultivate the government and blame yourself, stop evil in its first shoot and guard the sprouting, then baleful prodigies will melt away, harm be removed, and blessings gather."
translations.idiomatic: [2] Reform the administration, search your own heart, strangle misrule at the first green shoot, and calamity will lift while good fortune returns.

sentences.zh: 注[一]易曰「天垂象，見吉凶」，故言見戒也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] the Changes says "Heaven hangs images, showing good and ill"—therefore it speaks of "showing warning."
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Zhouyi says Heaven displays images to reveal fortune and misfortune—hence the phrase "shows a warning."

sentences.zh: 月滿先節謂未及望而滿也。

translations.literal: "Moon full before season" means it was full before reaching the expected full moon.
translations.idiomatic: "Full before its season" means the moon swelled to fullness ahead of the calendar.

sentences.zh: 東觀記亦*

translations.literal: The Eastern Lodge Record also *
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Lodge text also

translations.literal: (says)
translations.idiomatic: has an alternate reading here in the manuscript.

sentences.zh: *作「先節」，俗本作「失節」，字之誤也。

translations.literal: *writes "before season"; vulgar editions write "lost season"—an error in the graphs.
translations.idiomatic: The better reading is xian jie, "before its season"; popular texts miswrite it as shijie, "missed its season."

sentences.zh: 注[二]詩大雅也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] it is an ode of the Major Odes.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The quotation comes from the Major Odes.

sentences.zh: 雷電震耀，天怒也。

translations.literal: Thunder and lightning flash—Heaven's anger.
translations.idiomatic: Lightning and thunder are Heaven in anger.

sentences.zh: 戲豫猶逸豫也。

translations.literal: Xi yu is like ease and comfort.
translations.idiomatic: Xi yu means idle ease.

sentences.zh: 不敢自逸，所以敬天也。

translations.literal: Dare not self-ease—thereby one reveres Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: To shun complacency is to reverence Heaven.

sentences.zh: 夫壞崖破巖之水，源自涓涓；

translations.literal: Water that bursts cliffs and smashes rocks begins from tiny trickles;
translations.idiomatic: Floods that shatter cliffs begin as trickles;

sentences.zh: 干雲蔽日之木，起於□青。

translations.literal: trees that touch the clouds and hide the sun begin from tender mengqing shoots.
translations.idiomatic: forest giants that shade the sky begin as green shoots no wider than a finger.

sentences.zh: 禁微則易，救末者難，人莫不忽於微細，以致其大。

translations.literal: To check the small is easy; to rescue the end is hard—no man fails to slight the minute until it grows great.
translations.idiomatic: Evil is easy to stop at the bud and hard to uproot at maturity, yet everyone ignores the first faint signs.

sentences.zh: 恩不忍誨，義不忍割，去事之後，未然之明鏡也。

translations.literal: Favor cannot bear to reprove, duty cannot bear to sever—after the deed is done, foresight is only a mirror on what did not happen.
translations.idiomatic: When kindness spares correction and principle spares a clean break, hindsight is only a bright mirror on what might have been.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為左官外附之臣，[一]依托權門，傾覆諂諛，以求容媚者，宜行一切之誅。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks that "left-office" and outer-attached ministers [one] who lean on power gates, overturn and flatter to win favor ought to suffer summary execution.
translations.idiomatic: [1] I urge that every official who has slipped into a great clan's private service, who toadies for protection, be cut off without mercy.

sentences.zh: 閒者大將軍再出，威振州郡，莫不賦斂吏人，遣使貢獻。

translations.literal: Recently the great general twice went out; his might shook commanderies and counties; none failed to levy the people and dispatch envoys with tribute.
translations.idiomatic: Twice now the general has marched out; his prestige has cowed every commandery into squeezing the people for gifts sent through his couriers.

sentences.zh: 大將軍雖雲不受，而物不還主，部署之吏無所畏憚，縱行非法，不伏罪辜，故海內貪猾，競為奸吏，小民吁嗟，怨氣滿腹。

translations.literal: Though the great general says he does not accept, the goods do not return to their owners; the clerks he deploys fear nothing, recklessly act unlawfully and do not answer for guilt—therefore within the seas the greedy and crafty vie to be wicked clerks; commoners sigh and resentment fills their bellies.
translations.idiomatic: He says he takes no bribes, yet nothing is returned to the donors; his agents break the law with impunity, so knaves everywhere imitate them and the common people boil with grievance.

sentences.zh: 臣聞天不可以不剛，不剛則三光不明；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Heaven cannot but be firm; if not firm the three luminaries are not bright;
translations.idiomatic: Heaven must keep its firmness; without it sun, moon, and stars lose their light;

sentences.zh: [二]王不可以不強，不強則宰牧從橫。

translations.literal: [Two] the king cannot but be strong; if not strong shepherds and herdsmen run rampant.
translations.idiomatic: [2] the king must keep his strength; without it regional lords ride roughshod over the law.

sentences.zh: 宜因大變，改政匡失，以塞天意。

translations.literal: One ought to use this great anomaly to change government and correct failure, thereby to answer Heaven's intent.
translations.idiomatic: Use this celestial rebuke to reform policy and mend faults, and you will answer Heaven's demand.

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書：「左官附益阿黨之法設。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] the Former Book: "Laws against left-office, illicit enrichment, and partisan cliques were established.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Former Han history records statutes against serving great houses instead of the throne.

sentences.zh: 」左官者，人道尚右，捨天子而事諸侯為左官。

translations.literal: "Left-office" means: human custom esteems the right; abandoning the Son of Heaven to serve feudal lords is left-office.
translations.idiomatic: "Left-office" means leaving the emperor's service for a magnate's staff, reversing the proper precedence of right over left.

sentences.zh: 外附謂背正法而附私家。

translations.literal: Outer attachment means turning from correct law to cling to private families.
translations.idiomatic: "Outer attachment" means forsaking statute law to curry favor with private clans.

sentences.zh: 注[二]三光，日﹑月﹑星也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] the three luminaries are sun, moon, and stars.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The three luminaries are sun, moon, and stars.

sentences.zh: 天道尚剛。

translations.literal: Heaven's way esteems firmness.
translations.idiomatic: The way of Heaven is firmness.

sentences.zh: 周易曰：「干，健也。

translations.literal: The Changes says: "Qian is vigor."
translations.idiomatic: The Zhouyi says the creative hexagram Qian stands for strength.

sentences.zh: 」左傳曰：「天為剛德。」

translations.literal: " The Zuo commentary says: "Heaven has a firm virtue."
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo zhuan adds that Heaven's virtue is adamantine.'

sentences.zh: 書奏十餘日，帝以鴻行太尉兼□尉，屯南﹑北宮。

translations.literal: Ten-odd days after the memorial was submitted, the emperor made Hong act as grand commandant and concurrently colonel of the Northern Army, garrisoning the southern and northern palaces.
translations.idiomatic: Within a fortnight of Ding Hong's memorial the emperor named him acting grand commandant and colonel of the Northern Army, billeting him in the southern and northern palace compounds.

sentences.zh: 於是收竇憲大將軍印綬，憲及諸弟皆自殺。

translations.literal: Thereupon they seized Dou Xian's great general seal and ribbons; Xian and his brothers all killed themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Xian was stripped of the great general's seal and sash, and he and his brothers took their own lives.

sentences.zh: 時大郡口五六十萬舉孝廉二人，小郡口二十萬並有蠻夷者亦舉二人，帝以為不均，下公卿會議。

translations.literal: At that time great commanderies of five or six hundred thousand mouths each recommended two filial and incorrupt men; small commanderies of two hundred thousand that also had barbarians likewise recommended two; the emperor thought it uneven and ordered dukes and ministers to confer.
translations.idiomatic: Great commanderies of half a million souls sent up two filial-and-incorrupt candidates each, while tiny frontier prefectures with mixed populations had to match that quota; the emperor called the rule unfair and asked his ministers to redesign it.

sentences.zh: 鴻與司空劉方上言：「凡口率之科，宜有階品，蠻夷錯雜，不得為數。

translations.literal: Hong with Minister of Works Liu Fang submitted: "As for per-capita quotas, there ought to be grades; where barbarians are intermixed, they cannot be counted in the number."
translations.idiomatic: Ding Hong and Minister Liu Fang proposed graded quotas and insisted that non-Han populations not be lumped into the same head count as Chinese taxpayers.

sentences.zh: 自今郡國率二十萬口歲舉孝廉一人，四十萬二人，六十萬三人，八十萬四人，百萬五人，百二十萬六人。

translations.literal: From now commanderies and kingdoms: at twenty thousand mouths per year recommend one filial-incorrupt; forty thousand two; sixty thousand three; eighty thousand four; one million five; one million two hundred thousand six.
translations.idiomatic: Henceforth a commandery of twenty thousand adults would nominate one candidate yearly, forty thousand two, sixty thousand three, eighty thousand four, one million five, and one million two hundred thousand six.

sentences.zh: 不滿二十萬二歲一人，不滿十萬三歲一人。」

translations.literal: Under twenty thousand, one every two years; under ten thousand, one every three years."
translations.idiomatic: Below twenty thousand the nomination would come once every two years, and below ten thousand once every three years."

sentences.zh: 帝從之。

translations.literal: The emperor followed it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor adopted the plan.

sentences.zh: 六年，鴻薨，賜贈有加常禮。

translations.literal: In the sixth year Hong died; burial gifts exceeded the usual rites.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year of his reign Emperor He mourned Ding Hong with gifts richer than the ordinary standard.

sentences.zh: 子湛嗣。

translations.literal: His son Zhan succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Ding Zhan inherited the title.

sentences.zh: *[湛]*卒，子浮嗣。

translations.literal: *[Zhan]* died; his son Fu succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: When Ding Zhan died, his son Ding Fu succeeded.

sentences.zh: 浮卒，子夏嗣。

translations.literal: Fu died; his son Xia succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Fu was followed by his son Ding Xia.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]東觀記及續漢書「夏」字作「夔」也。

translations.literal: [One] Commentary [one] the Eastern Lodge Record and Continued Book of Han write the character Xia as Kui.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Note 1: Other histories write this heir's name with the graph kui instead of xia.

sentences.zh: 論曰：孔子曰「太伯三以天下讓，民無得而稱焉」。

translations.literal: The appraisal states: Confucius said "Taibo thrice yielded the empire; the people had no words to praise him."
translations.idiomatic: The historian reflects: Confucius said Taibo three times gave away the realm, yet the people could find no language fine enough to praise him.

sentences.zh: [一]孟子曰「聞伯夷之風者，貪夫廉，懦夫有立志」。

translations.literal: [One] Mencius said "Those who heard Boyi's wind, the greedy grew incorrupt, the weak resolved their will."
translations.idiomatic: [1] Mencius adds that Boyi's example turned the grasping honest and the timid resolute.

sentences.zh: 若乃太伯以天下而違周，伯夷率絜情以去國，並未始有其讓也。

translations.literal: As for Taibo, who left Zhou though the empire was his, and Boyi, who led pure intent to quit his state—they never began with a mind to "yield."
translations.idiomatic: Taibo walked away from a throne that could have been his, and Boyi fled Shang out of stainless principle; neither set out to win the reputation of a yielder.

sentences.zh: [二]故太伯稱至德，伯夷稱賢人。

translations.literal: [Two] Therefore Taibo is called utmost virtue, Boyi called worthy man.
translations.idiomatic: [2] That is why one is hailed as consummate virtue and the other as a sage recluse.

sentences.zh: 後世聞其讓而慕其風，徇其名而昧其致，所以激詭行生而取與妄矣。

translations.literal: Later ages, hearing of their yielding, admired their repute, chased the name and missed their true reach—thereby perverse conduct was stirred and giving and receiving grew reckless.
translations.idiomatic: Later men copied the gesture without the spirit, chasing the label of selflessness until sham yielding made every gift and bribe a masquerade.

sentences.zh: [三]至夫鄧彪﹑劉愷，讓其弟以取義，使弟受非服而己厚其名，於義不亦薄乎！

translations.literal: [Three] As for Deng Biao and Liu Kai, who yielded to younger brothers to seize "righteousness," making the brother receive unrighteous investiture while they thickened their own name—in principle is that not thin!
translations.idiomatic: [3] Think of Deng Biao and Liu Kai, who pressed titles on younger brothers to polish their own halo while trapping kin in titles they should never have held—that is a shabby sort of virtue.

sentences.zh: [四]君子立言，非苟顯其理，將以啟天下之方悟者；

translations.literal: [Four] The gentleman sets forth words not casually to display principle, but to awaken those in the world who should be enlightened;
translations.idiomatic: [4] A gentleman publishes doctrine not to parade cleverness but to rouse the obtuse;

sentences.zh: 立行，非獨善其身，將以訓天下之方動者。

translations.literal: sets conduct not only to perfect himself, but to instruct those in the world who should be moved.
translations.idiomatic: he models conduct not for private perfection alone but to teach everyone still wavering.

sentences.zh: 言行之所開塞，可無慎哉！

translations.literal: How can one not be careful where speech and conduct open or block the way!
translations.idiomatic: How grave is the duty when words and deeds can either open the road to goodness or wall it shut!

sentences.zh: 原丁鴻之心，主於忠愛乎？

translations.literal: Tracing Ding Hong's heart, did it rest in loyal love?
translations.idiomatic: Read Ding Hong's heart as loyal love for the dynasty:

sentences.zh: 何其終悟而從義也！

translations.literal: How he finally awakened and followed righteousness!
translations.idiomatic: see how he woke at a friend's rebuke and chose the harder right.

sentences.zh: 異夫數子類乎徇名者焉。

translations.literal: He differs from those several men who resemble the name-chasers.
translations.idiomatic: He stands apart from the crowd who only rehearsed yielding for applause.

sentences.zh: 注[一]此上論語載孔子之言也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one] this above is Confucius's saying in the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The quotation is from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 鄭玄注云：「太伯，周太王之長子，次子仲雍，次子季歷。

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary states: "Taibo was eldest son of Grand King Tai; the second son was Zhong Yong; the third was Ji Li.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan explains that Taibo was the eldest son of King Tai of Zhou, followed by Zhong Yong and Ji Li.

sentences.zh: 太王見季歷賢，又生文王有聖人表，故欲立之，而未有命。

translations.literal: Grand King saw that Ji Li was worthy and moreover bore King Wen with signs of a sage; therefore he wished to establish him, but there was as yet no command."
translations.idiomatic: King Tai saw worth in Ji Li and a sage's omens on the child who became King Wen, and longed to pass the realm to that line though no formal order had been given."

sentences.zh: 太王疾，太伯因適吳﹑越採藥，太王歿而不返，季歷為喪主，一讓也。

translations.literal: Grand King fell ill; Taibo therefore went to Wu and Yue to gather herbs; when Grand King died he did not return—Ji Li became chief mourner: the first yielding.
translations.idiomatic: When King Tai fell ill Taibo withdrew to the southeast on the pretext of gathering herbs and never returned for the funeral, leaving Ji Li to preside—the first renunciation.

sentences.zh: 季歷赴之，不來奔喪，二讓也。

translations.literal: Ji Li went to him; he did not come to mourn: the second yielding.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Li went to recall him, yet he would not return for the obsequies—the second renunciation.

sentences.zh: 免喪之後，遂斷髮文身，三讓也。

translations.literal: After mourning ended, he cut his hair and tattooed his body: the third yielding.
translations.idiomatic: After the mourning period he cropped his hair and tattooed his skin like a barbarian, making himself unfit to rule—the third renunciation.

sentences.zh: 三讓之美皆蔽隱不著，故人無得而稱焉。」

translations.literal: The beauty of the three yieldings was all hidden and not displayed; therefore people had no words to praise him."
translations.idiomatic: Because each act was done without thought of fame, ordinary praise could not compass his virtue.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]違，去也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] wei means depart.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Wei means "to leave" or "quit."

sentences.zh: 未始猶未嘗也。

translations.literal: wei shi is like wei chang—not yet.
translations.idiomatic: Wei shi here equals "never once."

sentences.zh: 言太伯﹑伯夷率性清絜，超然去國，未嘗故有求讓之名。

translations.literal: It means Taibo and Boyi followed their natures, pure and clean, transcendently left the state, never deliberately sought the reputation of yielding.
translations.idiomatic: Taibo and Boyi acted from innate purity, not to manufacture a reputation for selflessness.

sentences.zh: 注[三]徇，營也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] xun means pursue or seek.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Xun means to chase after.

sentences.zh: 言二子非故立讓風以求聲譽，故至德稱於前古。

translations.literal: It means the two men did not deliberately establish a yielding style to seek fame; therefore utmost virtue was praised in high antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: Because they never performed for an audience, antiquity could call their virtue complete.

sentences.zh: 後代之人直欲營慕其名，而昧其深致，所以激射詭譎之行生，而取與之閒多詐妄矣。

translations.literal: Men of later ages only wanted to pursue admiration of the name, missed their deep import—thereby deceitful conduct was shot forth between giving and receiving, and much fraud arose in what was taken and granted.
translations.idiomatic: Later imitators chased the label, missed the lesson, and turned gift and bribe alike into theater.

sentences.zh: 注[四]彪讓國異母弟荊及鳳，愷以國讓弟憲，帝皆許焉。

translations.literal: Commentary [four] Biao yielded the state to younger half-brothers Jing and Feng; Kai yielded the state to his brother Xian; in each case the emperor approved.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: Deng Biao ceded his place to half-brothers Jing and Feng, and Liu Kai to his brother Xian, with imperial approval.

sentences.zh: 弟不當襲爵，故言非服，而彪﹑愷皆獨受美名，而陷弟於不義也。

translations.literal: The younger brother ought not to have inherited the noble rank; therefore it is called unrighteous investiture—and Biao and Kai alone received fair name while trapping the brother in unrighteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Those brothers were not lawful heirs, yet Biao and Liu Kai walked off with the moral credit while saddling kin with titles they should not have held.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：五更待問，應若鳴鐘。

translations.literal: Encomium: The Elder of Fifth Renewal awaited questions, answering like a struck bell.
translations.idiomatic: Encomium: the ritual elder waited on the emperor's questions and answered like a bell when struck.

sentences.zh: [一]庭列輜駕，堂修禮容。

translations.literal: [One] In the courtyard stood covered carriages; in the hall ritual demeanor was cultivated.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Covered carriages lined his court; ritual filled his hall.

sentences.zh: 穆穆帝則，擁經以從。

translations.literal: Majestic were the emperor's norms; he embraced the classics to follow.
translations.idiomatic: August was the sovereign's bearing as he clutched the text and played disciple.

sentences.zh: [二]丁鴻翼翼，讓而不飾。

translations.literal: [Two] Ding Hong yiyi, yielding without adornment.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Ding Hong moved with cautious grace, yielding without show.

sentences.zh: 高論白虎，深言日食。

translations.literal: Lofty disputation at the White Tiger, deep words on the solar eclipse.
translations.idiomatic: He soared in debate at the White Tiger Hall and spoke trenchantly on the eclipse.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]禮記曰：「夙夜強學以待問。

translations.literal: [Three] The Record of Rites says: "Study hard morning and night to await questions."
translations.idiomatic: [3] The Liji says a scholar must study day and night against the moment the ruler will ask.

sentences.zh: 」又曰「善待問者如撞鐘，扣之以小者則小鳴，扣之以大者則大鳴，待其舂容而後盡其聲，不善荅問者反此」也。

translations.literal: " It also says: "He who treats questioners well is like striking a bell: tap lightly and it sounds small, tap hard and it sounds large; wait for its rolling resonance and then the sound is exhausted—those who poorly answer questions are the opposite."
translations.idiomatic: It also compares a good teacher to a bell: light questions draw a soft note, great questions a peal that rolls away only when the bronze has spent its voice.

sentences.zh: 注[二]從，就也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] cong means approach or follow.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Cong means "to attend" or "follow."

sentences.zh: 注[三]春秋經書「日有食之」。

translations.literal: Commentary [three] the Spring and Autumn classic records "there was a solar eclipse."
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: The Chunqiu annals record each eclipse with the formula "the sun was eaten."

sentences.zh: 杜注云：「日食者，月掩日。

translations.literal: Du's commentary says: "Solar eclipse means the moon covers the sun."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu explains that the moon passed over the face of the sun.

sentences.zh: 聖人不言月掩日，而以自食為文，闕於所不見也。」

translations.literal: The sage does not say the moon covers the sun but writes that the sun devoured itself—lacuna toward what could not be seen."
translations.idiomatic: The classic does not say "the moon masked the sun" but "the sun was eclipsed," acknowledging the limits of what the scribes could see.'

sentences.zh: 校勘記 一二四九頁三行桓榮字春卿按：集解引汪文台說，謂書鈔雲字子春。

translations.literal: Collation note: page 1249 line 3 "Huan Rong courtesy name Chunqing" — the Jijie cites Wang Wentai: the Shuchao gives the courtesy name as Zichun.
translations.idiomatic: Textual note: some editions give Huan Rong's courtesy name as Zichun rather than Chunqing.

sentences.zh: 一二四九頁三行事博士九江朱普按：王先謙謂今本東觀記作「朱文剛」。

translations.literal: Page 1249 line 3 "served erudite Zhu Pu of Jiujiang" — Wang Xianqian notes the Eastern Lodge text reads "Zhu Wengang."
translations.idiomatic: Textual note: the Eastern Lodge version of Zhu Pu's name is written Zhu Wengang.

sentences.zh: 一二五０頁一行入使授太子刊誤謂案文「入使」當作「使入」。

translations.literal: Page 1250 line 1 "entered envoy to instruct the heir" — Kanwu says the text should read "ordered him to enter."
translations.idiomatic: Textual note: an editor argues the phrase should read "ordered him into the palace to teach," not "entered envoy."

sentences.zh: 按：孔廣陶校注本北堂書鈔五十六引續漢書作「入授太子」，無「使」字。

translations.literal: Kong Guangtao's annotated Beitang Shuchao 56 cites the Continued Han as "entered to instruct the heir," without the word "envoy."
translations.idiomatic: Textual note: the Beitang Shuchao quotation omits the word "envoy."

sentences.zh: 張森楷校勘記謂治要無「使」字。

translations.literal: Zhang Senkai's collation notes that the Zhengyao likewise lacks "envoy."
translations.idiomatic: Textual note: the Tang encyclopedia Zhengyao also drops "envoy."

sentences.zh: 一二五０頁四行引閎弘為議郎按：東觀記無「弘」字。

translations.literal: Page 1250 line 4 "drew in Hong and Hong as gentlemen consultants" — the Eastern Lodge has no second "Hong."
translations.idiomatic: Textual note: the Eastern Lodge omits the second name in the phrase about Peng Hong and Xi Hong.

sentences.zh: 一二五０頁八行建武十八年夏旱汲本、殿本「十八年」作「十六年」。

translations.literal: Page 1250 line 8 "eighteenth year of Jianwu, summer drought" — Jiben and Dianben read "sixteenth year."
translations.idiomatic: Textual note: some block-print editions miswrite the drought year as the sixteenth of Jianwu.

sentences.zh: 按：光武紀建武十八年夏五月旱，是作「十六年」者誤也。

translations.literal: Checking the Annals of Guangwu, the summer drought of the eighteenth year of Jianwu in the fifth month shows that reading "sixteenth year" is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Textual note: Emperor Guangwu's annals confirm the drought in the fifth month of Jianwu 18, so "sixteen" is the error.

sentences.zh: 一二五０頁一一行戲弘字奉卿按：「奉」原斗「秦」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1250 line 11 "Xi Hong courtesy name Fengqing" — note: "Feng" was originally miswritten as "Qin"; corrected from Jiben and Dianben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the graph for "Feng" in Xi Hong's courtesy name was corrupted to "Qin" in some editions; the received text follows the palace block prints.

sentences.zh: 一二五０頁一一行吳郡人也按：張□謂吳郡順帝時置，榮時乃會稽郡耳，「郡」當為「縣」。

translations.literal: Page 1250 line 11 "man of Wu commandery" — note: Zhang (the surname is damaged in the received text) argues that Wu commandery was established in Emperor Shun's time; in Rong's day it was still Kuaiji commandery — "commandery" ought to be "county."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Xi Hong's home should read a county in Kuaiji, not Wu commandery, which did not yet exist in Huan Rong's lifetime.

sentences.zh: 一二五三頁五行食邑五千戶按：東觀記作「五百戶」，雲後以五更祿終厥身。

translations.literal: Page 1253 line 5 "food estate five thousand households" — note: the Eastern Lodge Record reads "five hundred households," saying afterward by the Elder of Fifth Renewal's salary he ended his life.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the Eastern Lodge gives five hundred households for Huan Rong's fief, explaining the income as the stipend of an elder of the fifth order.

sentences.zh: 一二五四頁五行則真邪幾於辨矣按：王先謙謂「真」蓋「貞」之誤。

translations.literal: Page 1254 line 5 "then true and deviant are nearly distinguished" — note: Wang Xianqian says "true" is probably error for "upright" (zheng).
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Wang Xianqian argues the graph zhen should read zheng, "straightforward," in this judgment sentence.

sentences.zh: 一二五四頁一０行並解見吳漢傳按：集解引黃山說，謂注誤，乃見公孫述傳。

translations.literal: Page 1254 line 10 "both explanations see Wu Han's biography" — note: the Jijie cites Huang Shan saying the commentary errs; they are seen in Gongsun Shu's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the cross-reference to Wu Han is wrong; the Yue Yang and Xi Ba stories belong rather with Gongsun Shu.

sentences.zh: 一二五五頁一行以侍中監虎賁中郎將按：刊誤謂漢無監虎賁官，蓋是「兼」字，與丁鴻同也。

translations.literal: Page 1255 line 1 "as palace attendant supervised Gentlemen-of-the-Household Rapid as Tigers" — note: Kanwu says Han had no office "supervise Rapid Tigers"; probably the graph is "concurrently," as with Ding Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the character should be "concurrently" rather than "supervise"; Han had no separate censorate over the Rapid-as-Tigers guard.

sentences.zh: 一二五五頁一四行則切而不勤按：集解引沉欽韓說，謂大戴禮保傅篇作「切而不攘」，賈誼傳及新書作「切而不媿」。

translations.literal: Page 1255 line 14 "cutting yet not diligent" — note: the Jijie cites Shen Qinhan saying the Great Dai Ritual chapter Baofu reads "cutting yet not encroaching"; Jia Yi's biography and the Xin shu read "cutting yet not ashamed."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: parallel texts in the Da Dai Liji and Jia Yi quote a different third word in this gloss on princely education.

sentences.zh: 一二五六頁一行召訓按：集解引惠棟說，謂本傳作「馴」，徐廣雲馴古訓字。

translations.literal: Page 1256 line 1 "summoned Xun" — note: the Jijie cites Hui Dong saying the biography writes the graph xun (horse); Xu Guang says that graph is the ancient form of xun, "to instruct."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the Eastern Lodge writes Shao Xun with the "horse" radical; Xu Guang notes it is the old form of the name meaning "instruction."

sentences.zh: 一二五八頁六行典執政無所迴避刊誤謂典為御史，非執政者，「政」當作「正」。

translations.literal: Page 1258 line 6 "Dian in holding government had nothing he avoided" — Kanwu says Dian was a censor, not one who "holds government"; "government" ought to be "uprightness."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: emend zheng, "integrity," for zheng, "government," since Huan Dian was an imperial censor.

sentences.zh: 按：御覽四二七引作「正」。

translations.literal: Checking: the Imperial Readings 427 citation reads "uprightness."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the Tang encyclopedia supports the reading "uprightness."

sentences.zh: 一二五九頁五行糲食醋餐按：聚珍本東觀記「醋餐」作「粗餐」。

translations.literal: Page 1259 line 5 "coarse grain and vinegar meals" — note: the Zhizhen Eastern Lodge edition writes "coarse meals" for "vinegar meals."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: one manuscript reads "coarse fare" instead of "vinegar fare."

sentences.zh: 一二五九頁六行時太守向苗按：校補引錢大昭說，謂鸞，沛國人，苗當為國相，桓典之為孝廉，國相王吉舉之，是其證。

translations.literal: Page 1259 line 6 "at that time prefect Xiang Miao" — note: the collation supplement cites Qian Dazhao saying Luan was a man of Pei; Miao ought to be state chancellor; Huan Dian's being recommended filial and incorrupt by state chancellor Wang Ji proves it.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the Eastern Lodge's "prefect" should be "chancellor of Pei," consistent with Huan Luan's home commandery and Huan Dian's filial-and-incorrupt sponsor.

sentences.zh: 此云「太守」，誤。

translations.literal: This saying "prefect" is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: The title "prefect" is therefore a scribal error.

sentences.zh: 一二五九頁七行復征*

translations.literal: Page 1259 line 7 "again summoned *
translations.idiomatic: Page 1259 line 7: lacuna in the phrase "again summoned and appointed gentleman consultant."

translations.literal: (bi)
translations.idiomatic: Editorial insertion: the lacuna wrongly added the graph bi, "summoned by a ministerial office."

sentences.zh: *拜議郎刊誤謂征則上征之，辟則諸府辟之，議郎當雲征而已，明多「辟」字。

translations.literal: *appointed gentleman consultant" — Kanwu says zheng is summons from the throne, bi is summons from the various offices; for gentleman consultant the text should read only zheng — clearly an extra bi graph.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Kanwu holds that the redundant bi should be deleted; the gentleman consultant post was filled by imperial summons (zheng), not by a yamen summons (bi).

sentences.zh: 今據刪。

translations.literal: Now accordingly deleted.
translations.idiomatic: The editors have removed the superfluous graph.

sentences.zh: 一二五九頁一０行*[除]*陳留巳吾長據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1259 line 10 "*[appointed]* magistrate of Yiwu in Chenliu" — supplied from Jiben and Dianben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the words "appointed magistrate of Yiwu" are restored from the palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一二六０頁六行一無所*

translations.literal: Page 1260 line 6 "one nothing *
translations.idiomatic: Page 1260 line 6: lacuna in "accepted nothing."

translations.literal: (ought)
translations.idiomatic: Gloss: the full phrase is yi wu suo shou, "accepted nothing."

sentences.zh: **[留]*據殿本改，與聚珍本東觀記合。

translations.literal: **[retain]*" — changed per Dianben, matching the Zhizhen Eastern Lodge.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the lacuna is filled as in the Dianben, agreeing with the Zhizhen Eastern Lodge.

sentences.zh: 一二六一頁三行*[猛]*雅善彬等據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1261 line 3 "*[Meng]* Ya deeply esteemed Bin and the rest" — supplied from Jiben and Dianben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the name Meng Ya is restored from the block-print tradition.

sentences.zh: 按：御覽二一五引重「猛」字。

translations.literal: Checking: the Imperial Readings 215 citation doubles the graph "Meng."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: one Tang quotation repeats the graph "Meng."

sentences.zh: 一二六一頁四行彬遂以廢按：御覽二一五引「以」作「見」。

translations.literal: Page 1261 line 4 "Bin thereupon by waste" — note: Imperial Readings 215 citation writes "see" for "by."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the Imperial Readings variant reads "was seen as" instead of "thereupon by."

sentences.zh: 一二六一頁六行所著七說按：校補引侯康及柳從辰說，並謂「七說」當作「七誤」。

translations.literal: Page 1261 line 6 "what he wrote Seven Discourses" — note: the collation supplement cites Hou Kang and Liu Congchen both saying "Seven Discourses" ought to be "Seven Errors."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: some scholars emend the title to "Seven Errors" rather than "Seven Discourses."

sentences.zh: 一二六一頁六行夙智早成岐嶷也按：刊誤謂案蔡邕本以早成為一德，傳寫之誤，反以「岐嶷」在下，當云「夙智岐嶷，早成也」。

translations.literal: Page 1261 line 6 "early wisdom completed, qi-yi" — note: Kanwu says examining Cai Yong's original, "early completion" was one virtue; copyists wrongly reversed so "qi-yi" came below — it should read "early wisdom and qi-yi, completion."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Kanwu argues Cai Yong's list paired "precocious" with "keen and knowing," not with "early completion."

sentences.zh: 一二六一頁一三行*[孔]*子曰據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1261 line 13 "*[The Master]* said" — supplied from Jiben and Dianben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the words "The Master said" are restored from the palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一二六二頁四行丁鴻字孝公按：王先謙謂李善文選注作「字季公」。

translations.literal: Page 1262 line 4 "Ding Hong courtesy name Xiaogong" — note: Wang Xianqian says Li Shan's Wenxuan commentary writes "courtesy name Jigong."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Li Shan's commentary gives the courtesy name as Jigong instead of Xiaogong.

sentences.zh: 一二六三頁七行九江人鮑駿按：集解引惠棟說，謂袁宏紀「駿」作「俊」。

translations.literal: Page 1263 line 7 "Bao Jun of Jiujiang" — note: the Jijie cites Hui Dong saying Yuan Hong's annals write "Jun" as "Jun" (talented).
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Yuan Hong writes the name with a different homophone.

sentences.zh: 一二六三頁一五行故言亂*

translations.literal: Page 1263 line 15 "hence says chaotic *
translations.idiomatic: Page 1263 line 15: lacuna after "chaotic."

translations.literal: (ye)
translations.idiomatic: Particle ye closes the gloss.

sentences.zh: **[世]*據殿本改。

translations.literal: **[age]*" — changed per Dianben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the lacuna is filled as "ages" per the Dianben.
